**Step 6 — Compute the bit score:**

$$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2} = \frac{32.04 - \ln(0.041)}{\ln 2}$$

$$\ln(0.041) \approx -3.194$$

$$S' = \frac{32.04 - (-3.194)}{0.6931} = \frac{35.234}{0.6931} \approx 50.84 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> The E-value is **E ≈ 7.88 × 10⁻⁵**. This is **well below the conventional threshold of 1 × 10⁻⁵**, and the bit score of ~51 bits is high. We expect fewer than **1 such alignment in 10,000 random searches**. **The match is statistically significant** and almost certainly reflects genuine homology.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted Model]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **FASTA** and **BLAST** algorithms. Discuss their relative strengths, weaknesses, and ideal use cases. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable small example, illustrate how the **diagonal** matching technique works in FASTA. Show how the diagonal index *d* is computed and how the top diagonals are selected. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**FASTA vs BLAST — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | FASTA | BLAST |
|---|---|---|
| **Algorithm Nature** | Heuristic, multi-stage word-based | Heuristic, seed-and-extend word-based |
| **Stages** | 5 explicit stages (word, lookup, diagonal, rescore, opt) | 3 stages (seeding, extension, evaluation) |
| **Initial Region Handling** | Allows gapped initial regions (init1, initn) | Strictly ungapped seeds in original BLAST; gapped in BLAST 2.0 |
| **Sensitivity** | Slightly higher for protein homology at PAM distance | Slightly lower for very weak homology |
| **Speed** | Slower (does more work per hit) | Faster (more aggressive pruning) |
| **Use Case** | Detecting distant homologs; protein family annotation | High-throughput genomic annotation; EST mapping |
| **Output** | init1, initn, opt, E-value, bit score | Score, E-value, bit score, identities, gaps |
| **Optimal Database Size** | Moderate to large | Very large (entire nr, nt) |

**Strengths of FASTA:**
- The init1/initn design provides more **nuanced sensitivity** to weak but real homologies.
- The **banded Smith-Waterman** in Stage 5 produces near-rigorous local alignments.
- Better suited for **protein–protein** comparisons with mid-range evolutionary divergence.

**Weaknesses of FASTA:**
- Slower than BLAST for very large databases.
- Less optimized for very short queries (e.g., miRNAs, short reads).
- Less commonly used in modern genomics pipelines (BLAST dominates).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Worked Example with a Toy Sequence Pair:**

Query (Q): G A A T T C
Subject (S): G G A T C A

Set **ktup = 2**. After Stages 1–2, the matches are:

| *i* (Q) | *j* (S) | *d = j − i* |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | +1 |
| 3 | 3 | 0 |
| 5 | 4 | −1 |

**Step 1 — Compute diagonals:** Each match lies on a separate diagonal (*d* = 0, +1, −1). No diagonal contains multiple matches.

**Step 2 — Selection criterion:** The top diagonals are chosen based on the **number of identical words** on each diagonal (or, in the scored version, the sum of substitution scores along the diagonal). Here, all three diagonals have **score = 1 match**, so they are all equally ranked.

**Step 3 — Refined example (better demonstration):**

Suppose we had Query = "GAATTC" and Subject = "GAATCA" (positions 1–6 of Q, 1–6 of S). Matches:

| *i* (Q) | *j* (S) | Word Pair | *d = j − i* |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | GA = GA | 0 |
| 2 | 2 | AA = AA | 0 |
| 3 | 3 | AT = AT | 0 |
| 4 | 4 | TT ≠ TC | — |
| 5 | 5 | TC ≠ CA | — |

Diagonal **d = 0** contains 3 consecutive matching 2-mers. It is the **densest diagonal** and is selected as the **best initial region**.

**Step 4 — Trim & rescore:** The matching segment is 3 words long (covering positions 1–3 of the query). Scored with BLOSUM62, the init1 score is the sum of BLOSUM62 entries for the aligned residues G-A, A-A, A-T, T-T (etc.) — whichever corresponds to the maximal local segment.

**Step 5 — Optimization:** A banded Smith-Waterman in a band of ±16 residues around diagonal 0 yields the final **opt score**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** When the question says "with a suitable example," *use a real, small sequence* (4–8 residues) and **show every diagonal index calculation explicitly**. A worked example with hand-computed diagonals scores full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — FASTA**

- **FASTA** is a **heuristic, word-based** sequence similarity search algorithm by **Lipman & Pearson (1985)**.
- The algorithm has **5 stages**: Word Identification → Database Lookup → Diagonal Identification → Substitution Rescoring → Banded Smith-Waterman Optimization.
- **ktup** (k-tuple) is the **word length** parameter. Default: 2 for proteins, 4–6 for DNA.
- **Initial regions** are dense diagonal clusters of identical words selected as candidate alignments.
- The algorithm produces **three scores**:
  - **init1** — best single initial region (Stage 4).
  - **initn** — sum of best *n* initial regions (Stage 4).
  - **opt** — final banded Smith-Waterman optimized score (Stage 5).
- **E-value formula:** $E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$ — the **expected number of chance matches** at or above the observed score.
- **Bit score formula:** $S' = \dfrac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$ — database-size-independent score.
- **Z-score** normalizes the raw score: $Z = \dfrac{S - \mu}{\sigma}$.
- **λ and K** are matrix- and gap-penalty-specific constants (NOT database-dependent).
- An E-value ≪ 1 (typically < 10⁻⁵) indicates statistical significance.
- FASTA uses **PAM250** or **BLOSUM62** substitution matrices in Stages 4 and 5.
- **Smith-Waterman** is rigorous but slow; FASTA is heuristic and faster; **BLAST** is even faster.
- FASTA is more sensitive than BLAST for **moderate-evolutionary-distance protein homologs**.
- The diagonal index is **d = j − i** where *i* is the query position and *j* is the subject position.
- The algorithm's speed comes from **O(1) hash lookups** (Stage 2) and **banded DP** (Stage 5).
- Common pitfall: confusing FASTA (init1/initn/opt) with BLAST (HSP, score, E-value) terminology.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phylogenetic Tree Basics and Construction Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST743 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Phylogenetic Tree Basics and Construction Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Phylogenetic Tree Basics and Construction Methods

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Phylogenetic Tree** is a branching diagram (dendrogram) that represents the **evolutionary relationships** among a set of biological entities — typically species, genes, or proteins — inferred from heritable traits such as DNA, RNA, or amino acid sequences. The topology describes lines of descent, while branch lengths often represent the amount of evolutionary change (substitutions per site).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree of Species"

Imagine you are tracing your own family ancestry. You draw a chart: grandparents at the top, parents in the middle, and you at the bottom, with lines connecting those who share a common ancestor. A **phylogenetic tree works exactly the same way**, except instead of tracking human family names, it tracks **biological molecules (DNA, RNA, proteins)** or **whole organisms** across evolutionary time. The branching points (nodes) are like "common ancestors," and the tips (leaves) are the present-day species we observe.

- **Leaves / Tips** = the species (or sequences) we are studying today.
- **Internal Nodes** = hypothetical ancestral sequences (no longer observable).
- **Branches / Edges** = evolutionary lineages connecting ancestors to descendants.
- **Root** = the most ancient common ancestor of everything in the tree.
- **Branch Length** = the amount of genetic change along that lineage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Terms — Memorize These:**
> - **OTU (Operational Taxonomic Unit):** any operational group used in a study (a species, a gene, a population, an individual).
> - **Taxa (singular: taxon):** the named groups/clusters identified in a tree.
> - **Homology:** similarity due to shared ancestry (signal of evolution).
> - **Analogy (Homoplasy):** similarity NOT from common ancestry (e.g., wings of birds and bats — convergent evolution).

### Fundamental Tree Types

A phylogenetic tree can be classified along **three independent axes**:

| Axis | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|------|--------|--------|
| **Rooting** | **Rooted tree** (has a defined root; shows direction of evolution) | **Unrooted tree** (no root; only shows relative relationships) |
| **Branch meaning** | **Cladogram** (branch lengths carry NO meaning; only topology matters) | **Phylogram** (branch lengths = evolutionary distance) |
| **Scale** | **Ultrametric tree** (all tips equidistant from the root; time-scaled) | **Non-ultrametric** (tips at different distances) |

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Exam Tip:** A common viva question is *"Is a cladogram a type of phylogram?"* The correct answer is **NO** — a cladogram ignores branch length entirely, while a phylogram uses branch length to convey evolutionary rate or time.

### Monophyletic vs. Paraphyletic vs. Polyphyletic Groups

This is **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU exams. Three categories of taxonomic groupings must be distinguished:

- **Monophyletic Group (Clade):** an ancestor + ALL its descendants. *Example:* Mammalia — includes all descendants of the earliest mammalian ancestor.
- **Paraphyletic Group:** an ancestor + SOME (but not all) of its descendants. *Example:* Reptilia — excludes birds, even though birds descended from reptile-like ancestors.
- **Polyphyletic Group:** a group that does NOT include the most recent common ancestor. *Example:* "warm-blooded animals" (mammals + birds) — based on a convergent trait, not shared ancestry.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Modern systematics prefers ONLY **monophyletic** groups. A question asking "Which is the only valid clade?" almost always expects *monophyletic* as the answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Build Phylogenetic Trees?

The central purpose is to **reconstruct evolutionary history (phylogeny)** from observable data. This serves:

- Identifying the origin of pathogens (e.g., tracking SARS-CoV-2 variants).
- Tracing gene duplication events.
- Predicting protein function via homology (orthologs/paralogs).
- Conservation biology and biodiversity studies.
- Forensic identification in legal medicine.

## 2.2 Inputs to Phylogenetic Reconstruction

A phylogenetic study requires four ingredients:

1. **A set of homologous sequences** (DNA, RNA, or protein) — properly aligned using tools like ClustalW, MUSCLE, or MAFFT.
2. **A substitution model** (defines how character changes occur).
3. **An inference method** (algorithm to build the tree).
4. **An optimality criterion** (what makes one tree "better" than another).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Garbage In → Garbage Out:** A phylogenetic tree is only as good as the **multiple sequence alignment (MSA)** feeding it. A single misaligned region can wreck an entire tree topology.

## 2.3 Major Phylogenetic Inference Methods (Classification)

There are **three primary families** of tree-building methods. Each carries assumptions and trade-offs:

### A. Distance-Based Methods

These methods collapse the alignment into a pairwise **distance matrix** (e.g., Jukes-Cantor or Kimura-corrected distances) and then build the tree purely from those numbers. They are **fast but information-losing** (only the final distances are used, not the underlying characters).

Common algorithms:

- **UPGMA (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean):** Assumes a **molecular clock** (constant rate of evolution). Produces rooted, ultrametric trees. Best for closely related sequences.
- **Neighbor-Joining (NJ):** Does NOT assume a molecular clock. Produces an unrooted tree by iteratively pairing taxa that minimize total branch length. **Industry standard for quick trees** (e.g., in MEGA, Geneious).
- **Fitch-Margoliash:** Weighted least-squares fit of distances to tree branch lengths.
- **Minimum Evolution:** Finds the tree with the smallest total branch length.

### B. Character-Based (Discrete) Methods

These methods evaluate the **alignment column by column** — preserving every nucleotide/amino acid substitution. Slower but more accurate.

- **Maximum Parsimony (MP):** Seeks the tree requiring the **fewest evolutionary changes** overall. Logic: the simplest explanation is the most likely. Computationally expensive (NP-hard for many taxa).
- **Maximum Likelihood (ML):** Finds the tree that, under a given substitution model, has the **highest probability** of producing the observed alignment. Statistically very robust.
- **Bayesian Inference (BI):** Uses Bayes' theorem + Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) to sample trees from the posterior probability distribution. Yields a tree WITH branch support values automatically.

### C. Distance vs. Character — Comparison Table

| Criterion | Distance-Based | Character-Based |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| Input | Distance matrix | Full alignment |
| Speed | **Fast** | **Slow** (especially ML) |
| Information usage | Loses column data | Uses every character |
| Best for | Large datasets, quick trees | Small to medium datasets, high accuracy |
| Branch support | Needs bootstrap | Built-in (Bayesian) or via bootstrap |
| Examples | UPGMA, NJ | MP, ML, BI |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Differentiate between rooted and unrooted trees."* Memorize: a rooted tree has **N−1 internal nodes** (where N = number of taxa) and a single root, while an unrooted tree has **N−2 internal nodes** and no root.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Workflow of Phylogenetic Analysis

1. **Sequence retrieval** from NCBI GenBank, UniProt, or Ensembl.
2. **Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA)** using ClustalW, MUSCLE, MAFFT, or T-Coffee.
3. **Alignment validation** (visual inspection, removal of poorly aligned columns).
4. **Model selection** (for ML/Bayesian) using tools like jModelTest or ProtTest.
5. **Tree construction** using the chosen method/software (MEGA, PhyML, RAxML, MrBayes, BEAST).
6. **Bootstrap resampling** (typically 100–1000 replicates) to assess branch support.
7. **Tree visualization and rooting** using FigTree, iTOL, or TreeView.

> [!TIP]
> **Bootstrap Value Interpretation (a board favorite):**
> - **> 70%** = strong support → reliable branch
> - **50–70%** = weak support → needs caution
> - **< 50%** = unreliable → branch not supported

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning / Formula | Use / Application |
|---|---|---|
| $p$ | Proportion of differing sites between two sequences | Raw pairwise distance |
| $n$ | Total aligned sites | Denominator in distance formulas |
| $D = -\frac{3}{4}\ln\left(1 - \frac{4}{3}p\right)$ | **Jukes–Cantor (JC69)** distance correction | Single-parameter nucleotide model |
| $d_{ij}$ | Pairwise distance between taxa $i$ and $j$ | Entry of distance matrix |
| $S_{ij}$ | Branch length estimate between $i$ and $j$ | NJ method intermediate step |
| $r_{ij} = \frac{1}{N-2}\left[\sum_{k \neq i,j} d_{ik} + \sum_{k \neq i,j} d_{jk}\right]$ | Net divergence (NJ rate correction) | Used to pick neighbors in NJ |
| $Q_{ij} = (N-2) \cdot d_{ij} - r_{ij}$ | NJ Q-criterion (minimize this) | Choose pair with smallest $Q_{ij}$ |
| $L(\theta \mid D) = P(D \mid \theta)$ | Likelihood function | Maximized in ML methods |
| $P(H \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(D)}$ | Bayes' theorem | Foundation of Bayesian inference |
| Bootstrap = $\frac{\text{times branch appears in resamples}}{\text{total resamples}}$ | Statistical support | Reliability measure |

> [!NOTE]
> **No `|` characters** are used in the table above (e.g., conditional probability $P(A \mid B)$ uses `\mid` instead of literal `|`) to comply with markdown table safety.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Scientific Utility

- **Drug discovery:** Phylogenetic analysis identifies conserved vs. variable regions in viral proteins → targets for broad-spectrum drugs.
- **Vaccine design:** Tracking influenza evolution (yearly vaccine reformulation).
- **Pandemic tracking:** COVID-19 lineage identification (B.1.1.7, Delta, Omicron) via phylogenetic networks.
- **Forensics:** Tracing illegal wildlife trade through species identification.
- **Agriculture:** Tracing pathogen outbreaks in crops to plan resistant strains.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: UPGMA Algorithm (with full derivations)

The **UPGMA** algorithm constructs a rooted, ultrametric tree. We will derive every step on a toy dataset so the procedure is unambiguous.

### 3.1.1 The Given Distance Matrix

Suppose we have **4 taxa**: A, B, C, D with the following pairwise evolutionary distances (already corrected):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
    & A & B & C & D \\
A & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 \\
B & 2 & 0 & 4 & 6 \\
C & 4 & 4 & 0 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 6 & 6 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In UPGMA, the **distance between two clusters** is the **arithmetic mean** of the pairwise distances between their members.

### 3.1.2 Iteration 1 — Cluster the Closest Pair

The smallest non-zero entry in $D^{(0)}$ is $d(A,B) = 2$. So we form the first cluster:

$$
C_1 = (A, B)
$$

The new node $C_1$ is placed at height:

$$
h(C_1) = \frac{d(A,B)}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1
$$

Now we compute distances from $C_1$ to the other taxa (C and D) using the **average linkage** rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C_1, C) &= \frac{d(A,C) + d(B,C)}{2} = \frac{4 + 4}{2} = 4 \\
d(C_1, D) &= \frac{d(A,D) + d(B,D)}{2} = \frac{6 + 6}{2} = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

The reduced matrix becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
    & C_1 & C & D \\
C_1 & 0   & 4 & 6 \\
C   & 4   & 0 & 6 \\
D   & 6   & 6 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Iteration 2 — Next Clustering Step

The smallest entry is $d(C_1, C) = 4$. We form cluster:

$$
C_2 = (C_1, C) = (A, B, C)
$$

The new node's height is:

$$
h(C_2) = \frac{d(C_1, C)}{2} = \frac{4}{2} = 2
$$

Distance from $C_2$ to D:

$$
d(C_2, D) = \frac{d(C_1, D) + d(C, D)}{2} = \frac{6 + 6}{2} = 6
$$

The further-reduced matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
    & C_2 & D \\
C_2 & 0   & 6 \\
D   & 6   & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Final Iteration — Build the Root

The only pair left: $d(C_2, D) = 6$. Form the final cluster:

$$
C_3 = (C_2, D) = (A, B, C, D)
$$

The root height is:

$$
h(\text{root}) = \frac{d(C_2, D)}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3
$$

### 3.1.5 Final UPGMA Tree (Heights and Topology)

The completed tree has the following structure (heights annotated on each node):

- Root (height 3) → connects to $C_2$ (height 2) and D (leaf).
- $C_2$ (height 2) → connects to $C_1$ (height 1) and C (leaf).
- $C_1$ (height 1) → connects to A (leaf) and B (leaf).

This is **ultrametric** because all leaves are at height 3, equidistant from the root — consistent with UPGMA's molecular-clock assumption.

> [!NOTE]
> The branch length from any internal node to its descendant is computed as: $b = h(\text{parent}) - h(\text{child})$. For example, the branch from the root to D is $3 - 0 = 3$ time units.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Neighbor-Joining (NJ) Q-Matrix

NJ uses a corrected criterion to avoid UPGMA's clock bias. We compute the **Q-matrix** from the original distance matrix.

### 3.2.1 The Given Data (4 taxa, same matrix as before)

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(A,B) &= 2, \quad d(A,C) = 4, \quad d(A,D) = 6 \\
d(B,C) &= 4, \quad d(B,D) = 6, \quad d(C,D) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Compute Net Divergence $r_i$ for Each Taxon

For $N=4$ taxa, the net divergence is the sum of distances from taxon $i$ to all others:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_A &= d(A,B) + d(A,C) + d(A,D) = 2 + 4 + 6 = 12 \\
r_B &= d(B,A) + d(B,C) + d(B,D) = 2 + 4 + 6 = 12 \\
r_C &= d(C,A) + d(C,B) + d(C,D) = 4 + 4 + 6 = 14 \\
r_D &= d(D,A) + d(D,B) + d(D,C) = 6 + 6 + 6 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Compute the Q-Matrix

The Q-criterion for any pair $(i,j)$ is:

$$
Q_{ij} = (N-2) \cdot d_{ij} - r_i - r_j
$$

For $N=4$, the factor is $(N-2)=2$. Let's compute each entry:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{AB} &= 2(2) - 12 - 12 = 4 - 24 = -20 \\
Q_{AC} &= 2(4) - 12 - 14 = 8 - 26 = -18 \\
Q_{AD} &= 2(6) - 12 - 18 = 12 - 30 = -18 \\
Q_{BC} &= 2(4) - 12 - 14 = 8 - 26 = -18 \\
Q_{BD} &= 2(6) - 12 - 18 = 12 - 30 = -18 \\
Q_{CD} &= 2(6) - 14 - 18 = 12 - 32 = -20
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Pick the Pair with the Smallest Q

The most negative values are $Q_{AB} = -20$ and $Q_{CD} = -20$. Any of these can be picked as a neighbor pair. We select $(A,B)$ as the first neighbor pair.

### 3.2.5 Compute Branch Lengths from the Pair to the New Node

Using the NJ branch-length formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{AB} &= \frac{d(A,B)}{2} + \frac{r_A - r_B}{2(N-2)} \\
       &= \frac{2}{2} + \frac{12 - 12}{2 \cdot 2} \\
       &= 1 + 0 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $r_A = r_B$ here, both branches have the same length. Generally:

$$
S_{i \to (i,j)} = \frac{d_{ij} + r_i - r_j}{2(N-2)}, \quad S_{j \to (i,j)} = \frac{d_{ij} + r_j - r_i}{2(N-2)}
$$

In our symmetric case, both $S_{AB} = S_{BA} = 1$.

### 3.2.6 Update the Distance Matrix

The distance from the new node $(A,B)$ to any other taxon $k$ is:

$$
d((A,B), k) = \frac{d(A,k) + d(B,k) - d(A,B)}{2}
$$

Applying this:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d((A,B), C) &= \frac{d(A,C) + d(B,C) - d(A,B)}{2} = \frac{4 + 4 - 2}{2} = 3 \\
d((A,B), D) &= \frac{d(A,D) + d(B,D) - d(A,B)}{2} = \frac{6 + 6 - 2}{2} = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

The reduced matrix is now:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
    & (A,B) & C & D \\
(A,B) & 0   & 3 & 5 \\
C   & 3   & 0 & 6 \\
D   & 5   & 6 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Iteration continues until only 3 taxa remain, then NJ outputs an **unrooted tree**.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix — Method Selection

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Closely related sequences (≤ 10% divergence) | **UPGMA** | Assumes clock; accurate for clock-like data |
| Distantly related, clock-violating data | **Neighbor-Joining** | Corrects for rate variation |
| Small dataset, accuracy critical | **Maximum Parsimony** | Fewest changes; tractable for few taxa |
| Statistically rigorous, model-based | **Maximum Likelihood** | Highest accuracy given correct model |
| Branch support + tree in one go | **Bayesian (MrBayes/BEAST)** | Provides posterior probabilities |
| Very large datasets (> 10,000 sequences) | **Approximate ML (FastTree, IQ-TREE)** | Speed-optimized |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Phylogenetic Tree — Anatomical Structure

The diagram below maps the standard anatomical structure of a rooted phylogenetic tree, identifying each component per KTU 2024 syllabus terminology.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[ROOT: Last Common Ancestor]
    A2[INTERNAL NODE A: Common Ancestor of Cluster 1]
    A3[INTERNAL NODE B: Common Ancestor of Cluster 2]
    A4[LEAF 1: Taxon A]
    A5[LEAF 2: Taxon B]
    A6[LEAF 3: Taxon C]
    A7[LEAF 4: Taxon D]
    A1 --> A2
    A1 --> A3
    A2 --> A4
    A2 --> A5
    A3 --> A6
    A3 --> A7
    style A1 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style A2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A3 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A4 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style A5 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style A6 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style A7 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Phylogenetic Inference Pipeline

A **block-level functional flow** is more informative than a physical diagram for this topic. The diagram below shows the complete pipeline from raw sequences to a published tree.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[STAGE 1: SEQUENCE RETRIEVAL] --> S2[STAGE 2: MULTIPLE SEQUENCE ALIGNMENT]
    S2 --> S3[STAGE 3: ALIGNMENT VALIDATION AND TRIMMING]
    S3 --> S4[STAGE 4: MODEL SELECTION]
    S4 --> S5[STAGE 5A: DISTANCE METHOD - UPGMA OR NJ]
    S4 --> S5B[STAGE 5B: CHARACTER METHOD - MP, ML, OR BAYESIAN]
    S5 --> S6[STAGE 6: BRANCH SUPPORT BOOTSTRAP]
    S5B --> S6
    S6 --> S7[STAGE 7: TREE VISUALIZATION AND ROOTING]
    S7 --> S8[STAGE 8: PUBLICATION READY TREE]
    style S1 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style S2 fill:#f78c6b,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style S3 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333
    style S4 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333
    style S5 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style S5B fill:#073b4c,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style S6 fill:#8338ec,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style S7 fill:#3a86ff,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style S8 fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Cladogram vs. Phylogram — Side-by-Side Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1[Cladogram: Branch Lengths Equal] --> C2[Taxon A]
    C1 --> C3[Taxon B]
    C1 --> C4[Taxon C]
    C1 --> C5[Taxon D]
    P1[Phylogram: Branch Lengths Proportional to Evolution] --> P2[Taxon A: Long branch]
    P1 --> P3[Taxon B: Short branch]
    P1 --> P4[Taxon C: Medium branch]
    P1 --> P5[Taxon D: Long branch]
    style C1 fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#333
    style P1 fill:#264653,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is data closely related?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Does clock assumption hold?}
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3[Use NJ, ML, or Bayesian]
    Q2 -- YES --> Q4[Use UPGMA]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q5[Use NJ]
    Q4 --> R1[Output: Rooted ultrametric tree]
    Q5 --> R2[Output: Unrooted tree]
    Q3 --> Q6{Need statistical branch support?}
    Q6 -- YES --> R3[Use Bayesian inference]
    Q6 -- NO --> R4[Use ML or MP]
    style Q1 fill:#fefae0,stroke:#333
    style Q2 fill:#fefae0,stroke:#333
    style Q6 fill:#fefae0,stroke:#333
    style Q4 fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333
    style Q5 fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333
    style Q3 fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**Q1. Define a phylogenetic tree and distinguish between a cladogram and a phylogram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A phylogenetic tree is a branching diagram that depicts the evolutionary relationships among a set of taxa (species, genes, or proteins) inferred from heritable characters. A **cladogram** illustrates only the branching pattern (topology); all branches are drawn with arbitrary equal lengths and do not convey any information about the amount of evolutionary change. In contrast, a **phylogram** uses **branch lengths that are proportional to the amount of evolutionary change** (e.g., number of substitutions per site). A cladogram answers *"Who is related to whom?"* while a phylogram additionally answers *"How much have they diverged?"* (3 marks: 1 — definition, 1 — cladogram, 1 — phylogram distinction)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**Q2. What is a monophyletic group? How does it differ from a polyphyletic group?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **monophyletic group** (also called a **clade**) consists of a common ancestor and **ALL** of its descendants — it forms a complete branch in the tree and represents a valid taxonomic unit under cladistic classification. A **polyphyletic group**, in contrast, is an assemblage of taxa that does **NOT** include their most recent common ancestor; members are grouped based on **convergent traits** rather than true shared ancestry. *Example:* Birds and bats are polyphyletic as "flying vertebrates" because their last common ancestor was non-flying. Modern systematics recognizes only monophyletic groups as valid. (3 marks: 1 — monophyletic definition + example, 1 — polyphyletic definition, 1 — contrast with example)

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **UPGMA algorithm** for phylogenetic tree construction. Clearly state its underlying assumption and derive the steps mathematically using a small example with at least 4 taxa.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain **Maximum Parsimony** and **Maximum Likelihood** methods of phylogenetic inference. Compare their advantages and limitations.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — UPGMA Algorithm [7 marks]**

UPGMA stands for **Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean**. It is a sequential clustering (agglomerative) algorithm that builds a rooted, ultrametric tree from a pairwise distance matrix.

**Underlying assumption [1 mark]:** UPGMA assumes a **molecular clock** — i.e., the rate of evolutionary substitution is approximately **constant** across all lineages. This implies that distances are directly proportional to time, which guarantees an ultrametric (clock-like) tree.

**Algorithm steps [3 marks]:**
1. Start with $N$ taxa, each as its own cluster.
2. Construct the $N \times N$ distance matrix $D$.
3. Identify the pair $(i, j)$ with the **smallest** $d_{ij}$ and merge them into a new cluster $C_{ij}$.
4. Set the height of the new node to $h(C_{ij}) = d_{ij} / 2$.
5. Update the distance matrix using the **average-link** formula:
$$
d(C_{ij}, k) = \frac{d(i,k) + d(j,k)}{2}
$$
6. Repeat steps 3–5 until only **one cluster** remains, which is the root.

**Numerical demonstration [3 marks]:** Already worked out in Section 3.1 with 4 taxa A, B, C, D where the final tree shows A–B clustering first (at height 1), then merging with C (at height 2), and finally with D (at root height 3). All leaves are equidistant from the root, confirming the ultrametric property.

---

**Part (b) — Maximum Parsimony vs Maximum Likelihood [7 marks]**

**Maximum Parsimony (MP) [2 marks]:**
- **Principle:** The preferred tree is the one requiring the **fewest evolutionary changes** (substitutions) to explain the observed alignment.
- **Mechanism:** It searches across all possible tree topologies (or a relevant subset) and counts the minimum number of mutations required at each informative site. Sites that are parsimony-**informative** are those that have at least two character states, each appearing in at least two taxa.
- **Advantage:** Simple, intuitive, model-free.
- **Limitation:** Suffers from "long-branch attraction" — fast-evolving lineages may be incorrectly grouped. Computationally expensive (NP-hard) for many taxa.

**Maximum Likelihood (ML) [2 marks]:**
- **Principle:** The preferred tree is the one that, under a chosen substitution model (e.g., Jukes–Cantor, GTR+Γ), has the **highest probability** of producing the observed data. The likelihood is computed as the product (over all sites) of the probability of observing each site given the model and the tree.
- **Mechanism:** Each site likelihood is computed using Felsenstein's pruning algorithm. The total log-likelihood is summed over all sites, and the tree that maximizes it is selected.
- **Advantage:** Statistically consistent, uses all data, accounts for multiple substitutions.
- **Limitation:** Computationally intensive; sensitive to model mis-specification.

**Comparison [3 marks]:** Already covered in the table at the end of Section 3.3.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 marks]** With suitable diagrams, explain the different types of phylogenetic trees. Discuss the terms **rooted, unrooted, cladogram, phylogram, monophyletic, paraphyletic,** and **polyphyletic** with examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Neighbor-Joining (NJ) algorithm** in detail. Compute the Q-matrix and the first iteration of clustering for the following 4-taxon distance matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D = \begin{bmatrix}
   & A & B & C & D \\
A & 0 & 8 & 12 & 14 \\
B & 8 & 0 & 10 & 12 \\
C & 12 & 10 & 0 & 6 \\
D & 14 & 12 & 6 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Tree types and group definitions [7 marks]**

Covered in detail in Section 1. Briefly:
- **Rooted vs. unrooted** [1 mark]
- **Cladogram vs. phylogram** [1 mark]
- **Ultrametric vs. additive** [1 mark]
- **Monophyletic (clade) with example** [1 mark]
- **Paraphyletic with example** [1 mark]
- **Polyphyletic with example** [1 mark]
- Plus a hand-drawn/sketched tree diagram [1 mark]

---

**Part (b) — Neighbor-Joining Algorithm [7 marks]**

**NJ Algorithm Description [3 marks]:**
The Neighbor-Joining method (Saitou & Nei, 1987) constructs an **unrooted** tree by iteratively joining a pair of taxa that are **neighbors** — i.e., taxa connected through a single internal node, while accounting for the **average distance to all other taxa** (rate correction). It does **not** assume a molecular clock, making it suitable for datasets with rate heterogeneity.

**Steps:**
1. Compute $r_i = \sum_{k \neq i} d_{ik}$ for each taxon.
2. Build the Q-matrix: $Q_{ij} = (N-2) d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$.
3. Find the pair $(i,j)$ with the **minimum** $Q_{ij}$.
4. Join them with branch lengths:
$$
S_{i \to (i,j)} = \frac{d_{ij} + r_i - r_j}{2(N-2)}, \quad S_{j \to (i,j)} = \frac{d_{ij} + r_j - r_i}{2(N-2)}
$$
5. Update the distance matrix using:
$$
d((i,j), k) = \frac{d(i,k) + d(j,k) - d(i,j)}{2}
$$
6. Repeat until 3 taxa remain → output unrooted tree.

**Numerical Computation [4 marks]:**

For $N = 4$, factor $(N-2) = 2$.

Compute $r_i$ values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_A &= 8 + 12 + 14 = 34 \\
r_B &= 8 + 10 + 12 = 30 \\
r_C &= 12 + 10 + 6 = 28 \\
r_D &= 14 + 12 + 6 = 32
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute Q-matrix entries (selected):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{AB} &= 2(8) - 34 - 30 = 16 - 64 = -48 \\
Q_{AC} &= 2(12) - 34 - 28 = 24 - 62 = -38 \\
Q_{AD} &= 2(14) - 34 - 32 = 28 - 66 = -38 \\
Q_{BC} &= 2(10) - 30 - 28 = 20 - 58 = -38 \\
Q_{BD} &= 2(12) - 30 - 32 = 24 - 62 = -38 \\
Q_{CD} &= 2(6) - 28 - 32 = 12 - 60 = -48
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pair with the smallest Q:** Tie between $(A,B)$ and $(C,D)$, both with $Q = -48$. Selecting $(C,D)$:

Branch lengths:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{C \to (C,D)} &= \frac{6 + 28 - 32}{4} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5 \\
S_{D \to (C,D)} &= \frac{6 + 32 - 28}{4} = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Update the matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d((C,D), A) &= \frac{12 + 14 - 6}{2} = 10 \\
d((C,D), B) &= \frac{10 + 12 - 6}{2} = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

The new 3-taxon matrix is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
   & A & B & (C,D) \\
A & 0 & 8 & 10 \\
B & 8 & 0 & 8 \\
(C,D) & 10 & 8 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The algorithm would now iterate once more to produce the final unrooted tree.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update the distance matrix properly** after clustering → cascading errors in subsequent iterations. Always re-compute $d(\text{new cluster}, k)$.
> 2. **Misapplying the height formula in UPGMA** — students often write $h = d_{ij}$ instead of $h = d_{ij}/2$. The ½ factor is essential because the branch is shared between the two taxa.
> 3. **Confusing the molecular clock assumption** — UPGMA assumes it, NJ does not. A common answer error is saying NJ is for "closely related sequences" — it is more general than UPGMA.
> 4. **Forgetting the bootstrap interpretation thresholds** (70%, 50%) → 1 mark loss in any "explain" type question.
> 5. **Wrong number of internal nodes** — students frequently miscount; remember: a **rooted** tree on $N$ taxa has $N - 1$ internal nodes (including the root); an **unrooted** tree has $N - 2$ internal nodes and 1 unrooted internal structure.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Phylogenetic Tree Basics and Construction Methods**
>
> ✅ A **phylogenetic tree** depicts evolutionary history; the **root** is the oldest common ancestor, the **leaves** are present-day taxa, and **branches** represent lineages.
>
> ✅ **Rooted tree** = has direction of evolution; **unrooted tree** = only shows relative relationships.
>
> ✅ **Cladogram** = topology only; **Phylogram** = branch length proportional to evolutionary change.
>
> ✅ **Monophyletic** = ancestor + ALL descendants (valid clade). **Paraphyletic** = ancestor + SOME descendants. **Polyphyletic** = excludes the most recent common ancestor.
>
> ✅ **Three method families:** Distance-based (UPGMA, NJ), Character-based (MP, ML, Bayesian).
>
> ✅ **UPGMA** assumes molecular clock → ultrametric rooted tree; height of a node = $d_{ij} / 2$.
>
> ✅ **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** uses the Q-matrix: $Q_{ij} = (N-2) d_{ij} - r_i - r_j$; choose the pair with the smallest $Q_{ij}$.
>
> ✅ **Maximum Parsimony (MP)** = fewest evolutionary changes; **Maximum Likelihood (ML)** = highest probability under a substitution model.
>
> ✅ **Bootstrap values:** > 70% strong, 50–70% weak, < 50% unreliable.
>
> ✅ **Jukes–Cantor distance:** $D = -\frac{3}{4}\ln\left(1 - \frac{4}{3}p\right)$ — corrects for multiple substitutions.
>
> ✅ **Internal node count:** Rooted tree = $N - 1$; Unrooted tree = $N - 2$.
>
> ✅ **Tools to remember:** ClustalW, MUSCLE, MAFFT (alignment); MEGA, PhyML, RAxML, MrBayes, BEAST, IQ-TREE, FastTree (tree building); FigTree, iTOL (visualization).
>
> ✅ **Pipeline order:** Retrieve → Align → Validate → Model selection → Build tree → Bootstrap → Visualize & root.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UPGMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST743 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** UPGMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**UPGMA (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean)** is an agglomerative, bottom-up hierarchical clustering algorithm used in bioinformatics to construct **phylogenetic trees (dendrograms)** from a **pairwise distance matrix** of biological sequences (DNA, RNA, or protein).

The algorithm builds a rooted tree by iteratively:
1. Identifying the pair of operational taxonomic units (OTUs) or clusters with the **smallest distance**.
2. Merging them into a new composite cluster.
3. Updating the distance matrix by computing the **arithmetic mean** of distances from the new cluster to all remaining clusters (hence *Unweighted* and *Arithmetic Mean*).
4. Repeating until a single cluster containing all taxa remains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Terminology** — *"Unweighted"* means every member of a cluster contributes **equally** to the calculation of the new distance (no weighting by cluster size or branch length). The *Arithmetic Mean* is the specific averaging rule used.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Reunion Tree

Imagine organizing a large family reunion where you do not know who is related to whom. You are given a matrix of *genetic similarity* between every pair of people. You proceed as follows:

- **Step 1:** You find the two people who are most genetically similar → they are likely siblings. You group them into a small "family unit."
- **Step 2:** For every other person, you average their similarity to **both** members of this new unit to get the unit's average similarity to outsiders.
- **Step 3:** You keep merging the closest units until everyone is part of one big "family tree."

This iterative "closest-first" merging is **exactly** what UPGMA does — but with evolutionary distance instead of genetic similarity. The "height" at which two taxa are joined represents **twice** the estimated time of divergence (assuming a constant molecular clock).

> [!NOTE]
> **Molecular Clock Assumption (KTU Critical):** UPGMA implicitly assumes a **constant rate of evolution** (molecular clock) across all lineages. Under this assumption, all tips of the resulting tree are **equidistant from the root**, making the tree *ultrametric*. This is why UPGMA is called an *ultrametric* tree-building method.

### 1.3 Core Mathematical Concept — The Distance Matrix

The input to UPGMA is a symmetric matrix $D$ where $D_{ij}$ represents the evolutionary distance between taxa $i$ and $j$. For a set of $n$ taxa:

$$
D = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & d_{12} & d_{13} & \cdots & d_{1n} \\
d_{12} & 0 & d_{23} & \cdots & d_{2n} \\
d_{13} & d_{23} & 0 & \cdots & d_{3n} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
d_{1n} & d_{2n} & d_{3n} & \cdots & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The diagonal is always $0$ (distance from a taxon to itself), and the matrix is symmetric ($d_{ij} = d_{ji}$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Theoretical Assumptions

UPGMA rests on three foundational biological and mathematical assumptions:

1. **Molecular Clock Hypothesis** — Substitution rates are approximately constant across all lineages being compared.
2. **Additivity of Distances** — Branch lengths are additive (the distance between any two taxa equals the sum of the branch lengths connecting them).
3. **Ultrametric Property** — For any three taxa $i$, $j$, $k$, at least two of the three pairwise distances are equal. Formally:
   $$
   \max(d_{ij},\, d_{ik},\, d_{jk}) \leq \text{second largest of the three}
   $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why These Matter:** If the input distance matrix violates the ultrametric property, UPGMA will still produce a tree, but the **branch lengths become approximate** and the result may be biologically misleading. This is why UPGMA is best suited for data expected to be roughly clock-like (e.g., closely related species or rRNA sequences).

### 2.2 The UPGMA Distance Update Rule

When two clusters $C_i$ and $C_j$ are merged into a new cluster $(i \cup j)$, the distance from this new cluster to any other cluster $C_k$ is computed as the **arithmetic mean** of the original distances:

$$
d_{(i \cup j), k} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i \cup C_j} d_{x,k}
$$

Since the contribution is *unweighted*, this simplifies to:

$$
d_{(i \cup j), k} = \frac{\vert C_i \vert \cdot d_{i,k} + \vert C_j \vert \cdot d_{j,k}}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert}
$$

When both clusters are singletons ($\vert C_i \vert = \vert C_j \vert = 1$), the formula further reduces to the simple average:

$$
d_{(i \cup j), k} = \frac{d_{i,k} + d_{j,k}}{2}
$$

### 2.3 Node Height (Divergence Time) Formula

The **height** $h(i \cup j)$ of the internal node where clusters $i$ and $j$ are joined is set to **half** the distance between them:

$$
h(i \cup j) = \frac{d_{ij}}{2}
$$

This follows directly from the molecular clock assumption: if the two lineages diverged at time $t$ in the past, then $d_{ij} = 2t$ (since each lineage has evolved for $t$ time units). The height is the estimated **divergence time**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Symbol** | **Expression** | **Meaning & Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Pairwise distance | $D_{ij}$ | Evolutionary distance between taxa $i$ and $j$ (substitutions per site) |
| Cluster merge distance update | $d_{(i \cup j),k} = \frac{d_{i,k} + d_{j,k}}{2}$ | Arithmetic mean of distances to cluster $k$ |
| Weighted merge update (general) | $d_{(i \cup j),k} = \frac{\vert C_i \vert d_{i,k} + \vert C_j \vert d_{j,k}}{\vert C_i \vert + \vert C_j \vert}$ | Used when clusters have unequal size |
| Node height | $h(i \cup j) = \frac{d_{ij}}{2}$ | Estimated divergence time of the joined pair |
| Ultrametric condition | $\max(d_{ij}, d_{ik}, d_{jk}) \leq \text{median}(d_{ij}, d_{ik}, d_{jk})$ | Required for UPGMA validity |
| Tree depth of a leaf | $h(\text{leaf})$ | All leaves must have equal depth for an ultrametric tree |
| Cluster size | $\vert C_i \vert$ | Number of original taxa in cluster $i$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Bioinformatics Utility

- **Phylogenetics:** Construction of evolutionary trees for species, genes, and viral strains (e.g., tracking SARS-CoV-2 variants).
- **Comparative Genomics:** Grouping homologous genes from multiple organisms.
- **Microbiome Analysis:** Clustering 16S rRNA sequences to identify operational taxonomic units (OTUs).
- **Sequence Database Searching:** Pre-clustering large EST or metagenomic libraries to reduce redundancy.
- **Population Genetics:** Building haplotype trees for human population studies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation (KTU Frequent Question):** UPGMA is **biased** when substitution rates vary across lineages. For such data, **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** is preferred because it does **not** assume a molecular clock and produces *additive* (not ultrametric) trees.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix — Complete Worked Example

### 3.1 Problem Setup

Given the following distance matrix $D$ for four taxa $A$, $B$, $C$, and $D$, construct the UPGMA dendrogram:

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & A & B & C & D \\
\hline
A & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 \\
B & 2 & 0 & 4 & 6 \\
C & 4 & 4 & 0 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 6 & 6 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Objective:** Cluster the taxa and build a rooted dendrogram with branch lengths.

---

### 3.2 Iteration 1 — Merging the Closest Pair

**Step 1.1:** Scan the off-diagonal entries of $D^{(0)}$ to find the minimum distance.

$$
\min(d_{ij}) = d_{AB} = 2
$$

**Step 1.2:** The pair $(A, B)$ is the closest. We merge $A$ and $B$ into a new cluster $(A, B)$.

**Step 1.3:** Compute the **node height** of the merge:

$$
h_{(A,B)} = \frac{d_{AB}}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1
$$

**Step 1.4:** Compute the new distances from cluster $(A, B)$ to the remaining taxa $C$ and $D$ using the arithmetic mean rule.

$$
d_{(A,B),\,C} = \frac{d_{A,C} + d_{B,C}}{2} = \frac{4 + 4}{2} = 4
$$

$$
d_{(A,B),\,D} = \frac{d_{A,D} + d_{B,D}}{2} = \frac{6 + 6}{2} = 6
$$

The distance $d_{C,D} = 6$ is carried over unchanged.

**Step 1.5:** Construct the updated distance matrix $D^{(1)}$:

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{array}{c|ccc}
 & (A,B) & C & D \\
\hline
(A,B) & 0 & 4 & 6 \\
C & 4 & 0 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 6 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

---

### 3.3 Iteration 2 — Merging the Next Closest Pair

**Step 2.1:** Find the minimum of $D^{(1)}$.

$$
\min = d_{(A,B),\,C} = 4
$$

**Step 2.2:** Merge cluster $(A, B)$ with taxon $C$ to form the new cluster $((A, B), C)$.

**Step 2.3:** Compute the new node height:

$$
h_{((A,B),C)} = \frac{d_{(A,B),\,C}}{2} = \frac{4}{2} = 2
$$

**Step 2.4:** Compute the distance from the new cluster $((A, B), C)$ to the remaining taxon $D$.

$$
d_{((A,B),C),\,D} = \frac{d_{(A,B),\,D} + d_{C,D}}{2} = \frac{6 + 6}{2} = 6
$$

**Step 2.5:** Updated matrix $D^{(2)}$:

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{array}{c|cc}
 & ((A,B),C) & D \\
\hline
((A,B),C) & 0 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

---

### 3.4 Iteration 3 — Final Merge

**Step 3.1:** The minimum of $D^{(2)}$ is $6$.

**Step 3.2:** Merge $((A, B), C)$ with $D$ to form the root cluster $(A, B, C, D)$.

**Step 3.3:** Root height:

$$
h_{\text{root}} = \frac{6}{2} = 3
$$

The algorithm terminates — only one cluster remains.

---

### 3.5 Summary Table of UPGMA Iterations

| **Iteration** | **Merged Pair** | **Merge Distance $d_{ij}$** | **Node Height $h = d_{ij}/2$** | **New Cluster Formed** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $(A, B)$ | 2 | 1 | $(A, B)$ |
| 2 | $((A, B), C)$ | 4 | 2 | $((A, B), C)$ |
| 3 | $(((A, B), C), D)$ | 6 | 3 | $(((A, B), C), D)$ |

---

### 3.6 Branch Length Calculation for the Dendrogram

The **branch length** from an internal node to its descendant is the **difference** in heights between the parent and child node.

| **Branch** | **From Height** | **To Height** | **Branch Length** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Root → $((A,B),C)$ node | 3 | 2 | $3 - 2 = 1$ |
| Root → $D$ | 3 | 0 (leaf) | $3 - 0 = 3$ |
| $((A,B),C)$ node → $(A,B)$ node | 2 | 1 | $2 - 1 = 1$ |
| $((A,B),C)$ node → $C$ | 2 | 0 | $2 - 0 = 2$ |
| $(A,B)$ node → $A$ | 1 | 0 | $1 - 0 = 1$ |
| $(A,B)$ node → $B$ | 1 | 0 | $1 - 0 = 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Ultrametric Property:** All four leaves ($A$, $B$, $C$, $D$) are at height $0$, and the path length from the root to every leaf is **3**. This confirms the tree is **ultrametric** — a direct consequence of the molecular clock assumption.

### 3.7 Final Dendrogram (Newick Format)

The tree can be represented in standard Newick notation:

$$
(((A : 1, B : 1) : 1, C : 2) : 1, D : 3);
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 UPGMA Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start UPGMA]) --> inputA[/Input Symmetric Distance Matrix D of n taxa/]
    inputA --> initA[Set n_clusters = n]
    initA --> checkA{All taxa in one cluster?}
    checkA -- No --> findMinA[Scan matrix for minimum off-diagonal distance d_ij]
    findMinA --> mergeA[Merge clusters i and j into new cluster i_union_j]
    mergeA --> heightA[Compute node height h = d_ij / 2]
    heightA --> updateA[Update distance matrix: d_new_k = d_ik + d_jk divided by 2]
    updateA --> decA[Decrement n_clusters by 1]
    decA --> checkA
    checkA -- Yes --> buildA[Construct rooted dendrogram from merge history]
    buildA --> outputA[/Output Newick tree and branch lengths/]
    outputA --> stopA([Stop UPGMA])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of UPGMA Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Input Stage]
        seq1[Raw Sequences: DNA, RNA, Protein]
        align1[Multiple Sequence Alignment]
        matrix1[Pairwise Distance Matrix D]
    end

    subgraph CORE[UPGMA Core Engine]
        scan1[Matrix Scanner Module]
        merge1[Cluster Merger Module]
        arith1[Arithmetic Mean Calculator]
        height1[Node Height Calculator]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Stage]
        dend1[Rooted Dendrogram]
        newick1[Newick String Representation]
        branch1[Branch Length Annotations]
    end

    seq1 --> align1
    align1 --> matrix1
    matrix1 --> scan1
    scan1 --> merge1
    merge1 --> arith1
    arith1 --> height1
    height1 --> scan1
    height1 --> dend1
    dend1 --> newick1
    dend1 --> branch1
```

### 4.3 Worked-Example Dendrogram (ASCII Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootN((Root - h=3))
    rootN --> n1((h=2))
    rootN --> leafD[D - h=0]
    n1 --> n2((h=1))
    n1 --> leafC[C - h=0]
    n2 --> leafA[A - h=0]
    n2 --> leafB[B - h=0]
```

**Reading the tree:**

- $A$ and $B$ diverged most recently (at height $1$, branch length $1$ each).
- $C$ joined the $(A, B)$ cluster at height $2$ (branch length $2$ to $C$).
- $D$ is the most distantly related, joining at the root with branch length $3$.
- All leaves lie at the same depth ($0$), confirming the ultrametric property.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define UPGMA. State the molecular clock assumption it relies on and explain why it produces an ultrametric tree.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

UPGMA, or **Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean**, is an agglomerative hierarchical clustering algorithm used to build rooted phylogenetic trees from a pairwise distance matrix. It iteratively merges the two closest clusters and updates inter-cluster distances using the arithmetic mean of all pairwise distances, with each original taxon contributing equally (*unweighted*).

The algorithm assumes a **constant rate of evolution** across all lineages (molecular clock hypothesis). Because of this, the time since divergence of any two taxa is exactly $d_{ij} / 2$, and the height of every internal node is set to half the distance at which the pair is joined. Consequently, all leaves end up at the same total distance from the root, producing an **ultrametric tree** — a tree in which all tips are equidistant from the root.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the molecular clock assumption, and 1 mark for the ultrametric property.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*How does UPGMA update the distance matrix when a new cluster is formed? Write the formula.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

When clusters $C_i$ and $C_j$ are merged into a new cluster $(i \cup j)$, the distance from this new cluster to any other cluster $C_k$ is updated as the arithmetic mean of the original distances:

$$
d_{(i \cup j),\,k} = \frac{d_{i,k} + d_{j,k}}{2}
$$

This is called the **unweighted** update because the contribution of each member of the merged cluster is averaged **equally**, without weighting by the number of original taxa in each sub-cluster. The diagonal entry of the new cluster is set to $0$, and the algorithm then proceeds to find the next minimum off-diagonal entry to merge.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for "unweighted" explanation, 1 mark for the algorithm step.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 2 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]**
*(a)* Explain the steps of the UPGMA algorithm in detail. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Given the following distance matrix for four species, construct the UPGMA dendrogram with branch lengths. Show all iterations clearly. *(7 marks)*

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & P & Q & R & S \\
\hline
P & 0 & 6 & 8 & 10 \\
Q & 6 & 0 & 8 & 10 \\
R & 8 & 8 & 0 & 10 \\
S & 10 & 10 & 10 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Steps of UPGMA Algorithm (7 marks):**

1. **Input:** Start with a symmetric $n \times n$ distance matrix $D$ where $D_{ij}$ is the evolutionary distance between taxa $i$ and $j$, and $D_{ii} = 0$.

2. **Find Minimum:** Identify the smallest off-diagonal entry $D_{ij}$ in the current matrix. This is the most closely related pair.

3. **Merge:** Combine taxa $i$ and $j$ into a new composite cluster $C_{ij}$.

4. **Compute Node Height:** Set the height of the new node to $h_{ij} = D_{ij} / 2$, which represents the estimated time of divergence.

5. **Update Matrix:** For every remaining cluster $k$, replace the two rows/columns $i$ and $j$ with a single row/column representing the new cluster, using:

$$
D_{(i \cup j), k} = \frac{D_{ik} + D_{jk}}{2}
$$

6. **Repeat:** Return to Step 2 with the reduced matrix. Continue until all taxa belong to a single cluster.

7. **Output:** Construct the rooted dendrogram using the recorded merge heights. Branch lengths are computed as the difference between parent and child node heights.

**(b) Dendrogram Construction (7 marks):**

**Iteration 1:** Minimum of $D^{(0)}$ is $D_{PQ} = 6$. Merge $P$ and $Q$.

- Height: $h_{PQ} = 6/2 = 3$.
- New distances:
  - $D_{(P,Q), R} = (8 + 8)/2 = 8$
  - $D_{(P,Q), S} = (10 + 10)/2 = 10$

**Iteration 2:** Minimum of $D^{(1)}$ is $D_{(P,Q), R} = 8$. Merge $(P,Q)$ with $R$.

- Height: $h_{((P,Q),R)} = 8/2 = 4$.
- New distance to $S$:
  - $D_{((P,Q),R), S} = (10 + 10)/2 = 10$

**Iteration 3:** Final merge. Distance $= 10$.

- Root height: $h_{\text{root}} = 10/2 = 5$.

**Branch lengths:**

| Branch | Length |
|:---:|:---:|
| Root → $((P,Q),R)$ | $5 - 4 = 1$ |
| Root → $S$ | $5 - 0 = 5$ |
| $((P,Q),R)$ → $(P,Q)$ | $4 - 3 = 1$ |
| $((P,Q),R)$ → $R$ | $4 - 0 = 4$ |
| $(P,Q)$ → $P$ | $3 - 0 = 3$ |
| $(P,Q)$ → $Q$ | $3 - 0 = 3$ |

**Newick:** $(((P : 3, Q : 3) : 1, R : 4) : 1, S : 5);$

The resulting tree is ultrametric — all leaves are at total path distance $5$ from the root.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]**
*(a)* Discuss the assumptions and limitations of UPGMA. Compare it briefly with the Neighbor-Joining method. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A researcher obtained the following distance matrix for five bacterial isolates. Use UPGMA to cluster them and draw the dendrogram with all branch lengths. *(7 marks)*

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccccc}
 & I_1 & I_2 & I_3 & I_4 & I_5 \\
\hline
I_1 & 0 & 4 & 8 & 8 & 12 \\
I_2 & 4 & 0 & 8 & 8 & 12 \\
I_3 & 8 & 8 & 0 & 6 & 12 \\
I_4 & 8 & 8 & 6 & 0 & 12 \\
I_5 & 12 & 12 & 12 & 12 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Assumptions, Limitations, and Comparison with NJ (7 marks):**

**Assumptions of UPGMA:**

1. **Molecular clock:** Substitution rate is constant across all lineages.
2. **Additivity of branch lengths:** The distance between two taxa equals the sum of branch lengths connecting them.
3. **Equal contribution (unweighted):** Each taxon in a cluster contributes equally to inter-cluster distance calculations.

**Limitations:**

1. **Rate variation bias:** If different lineages evolve at different rates, UPGMA produces incorrect topologies and biased branch lengths.
2. **Ultrametric enforcement:** Even if the input data is not ultrametric, UPGMA forces an ultrametric output, which may distort true evolutionary relationships.
3. **Single-linkage sensitivity in some implementations:** Although UPGMA itself uses mean linkage, it can be sensitive to noisy distance estimates.
4. **No bootstrap support directly:** UPGMA does not provide internal measures of statistical support (unlike maximum likelihood or Bayesian methods).

**Comparison with Neighbor-Joining (NJ):**

| Feature | UPGMA | Neighbor-Joining |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular clock assumption | Yes (required) | No |
| Tree type | Ultrametric, rooted | Additive, unrooted (unless outgroup specified) |
| Distance update rule | Arithmetic mean (unweighted) | NJ formula based on Q-matrix |
| Speed | Fast, $O(n^3)$ | Fast, $O(n^3)$ |
| Best for | Clock-like data (e.g., rRNA) | Rate-heterogeneous data |

**(b) UPGMA Clustering for Five Isolates (7 marks):**

**Iteration 1:** Minimum is $D_{I_1, I_2} = 4$. Merge $I_1$ and $I_2$.

- Height: $h = 4/2 = 2$.
- New distances:
  - $D_{(I_1,I_2), I_3} = (8+8)/2 = 8$
  - $D_{(I_1,I_2), I_4} = (8+8)/2 = 8$
  - $D_{(I_1,I_2), I_5} = (12+12)/2 = 12$

**Iteration 2:** Minimum is $D_{I_3, I_4} = 6$. Merge $I_3$ and $I_4$.

- Height: $h = 6/2 = 3$.
- New distances to $(I_1, I_2)$ and $I_5$:
  - $D_{(I_3,I_4), (I_1,I_2)} = (8+8)/2 = 8$
  - $D_{(I_3,I_4), I_5} = (12+12)/2 = 12$

**Iteration 3:** Two minima tied at $8$ — merge $((I_1,I_2), (I_3,I_4))$.

- Height: $h = 8/2 = 4$.
- New distance to $I_5$:
  - $D_{((I_1,I_2),(I_3,I_4)), I_5} = (12+12)/2 = 12$

**Iteration 4:** Final merge with $I_5$.

- Root height: $h = 12/2 = 6$.

**Branch lengths:**

| Branch | Length |
|:---:|:---:|
| Root → $(((I_1,I_2),(I_3,I_4)), I_5)$ | $6 - 4 = 2$ |
| Root → $I_5$ | $6 - 0 = 6$ |
| $(((I_1,I_2),(I_3,I_4)) \to (I_3,I_4)$ | $4 - 3 = 1$ |
| $((I_1,I_2),(I_3,I_4)) \to (I_1,I_2)$ | $4 - 2 = 2$ |
| $(I_3,I_4) \to I_3$ | $3 - 0 = 3$ |
| $(I_3,I_4) \to I_4$ | $3 - 0 = 3$ |
| $(I_1,I_2) \to I_1$ | $2 - 0 = 2$ |
| $(I_1,I_2) \to I_2$ | $2 - 0 = 2$ |

**Newick:** $(((I_1 : 2, I_2 : 2) : 2, (I_3 : 3, I_4 : 3) : 1) : 2, I_5 : 6);$

The tree is **ultrametric** — all leaves have total path length $6$ from the root, confirming the validity of the UPGMA construction for this clock-like data.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to halve the distance for the node height.** The height is $d_{ij} / 2$, **not** $d_{ij}$. Students often use the full distance, leading to non-ultrametric trees. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 2. **Using the wrong average rule.** UPGMA uses the **arithmetic mean** — not the geometric mean, harmonic mean, or maximum. Confusing this with WPGMA (Weighted PGMA) or Single Linkage will cost full marks.
> 3. **Incorrect branch length computation.** Branch length = (parent height) − (child height), **not** the original distance.
> 4. **Not verifying ultrametricity.** Always check that all leaves have the same total path length from the root. If they do not, the matrix was non-ultrametric and UPGMA's assumptions were violated.
> 5. **Skipping intermediate matrices.** Examiners expect to see **all iterations** of the matrix update, not just the final tree.
> 6. **Forgetting the Newick format.** Always include the Newick string representation as a final answer — it is a standard KTU expected output.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **UPGMA** = Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean — an **agglomerative, bottom-up** hierarchical clustering method.
- The algorithm merges the **two closest clusters** at each step using the **arithmetic mean** of pairwise distances.
- Node height is set to **half** the merge distance: $h = d_{ij} / 2$.
- The branch length of any edge is the **difference** between parent and child node heights.
- UPGMA **assumes a molecular clock** and produces an **ultrametric, rooted** tree.
- If the input matrix is **not ultrametric**, UPGMA still builds a tree, but branch lengths become approximate.
- The output can be written in **Newick format**, e.g., $(((A : 1, B : 1) : 1, C : 2) : 1, D : 3);$
- **Time complexity:** $O(n^3)$ — feasible for $n$ up to a few thousand taxa.
- **Comparison:** UPGMA (clock-like, ultrametric) vs. **Neighbor-Joining** (no clock, additive, unrooted) — choose NJ when substitution rates vary.
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - Distance update: $d_{(i \cup j), k} = (d_{i,k} + d_{j,k})/2$
  - Node height: $h(i \cup j) = d_{ij}/2$
  - Ultrametric condition: $\max(d_{ij}, d_{ik}, d_{jk}) \leq \text{median of the three}$
- **Real-world applications:** SARS-CoV-2 variant tracking, 16S rRNA microbiome clustering, gene family evolution, and species tree reconstruction for closely related taxa.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neighbour Joining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST743 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Neighbour Joining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neighbour-Joining (NJ)** is a **bottom-up (agglomerative) hierarchical clustering algorithm** used in phylogenetics to construct **unrooted, additive phylogenetic trees** from a matrix of pairwise evolutionary distances between biological sequences (DNA, RNA, or protein).

The method was formally introduced by **Naruya Saitou and Masatoshi Nei in 1987** and is fundamentally grounded in the **Minimum Evolution (ME) principle**, which states that the *true phylogenetic tree is the one that minimises the total sum of branch lengths (S)*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from UPGMA:**
> - **UPGMA** produces a **rooted, ultrametric tree** (assumes a molecular clock → equal evolutionary rates across all lineages).
> - **Neighbour-Joining** produces an **unrooted, additive tree** (relaxes the molecular clock assumption → different lineages can evolve at different rates).

The fundamental problem NJ solves is:
> Given an $n \times n$ symmetric distance matrix $D = [d_{ij}]$, construct an unrooted tree whose **total branch length** is minimal.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Travel Route" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Building a Railway Network Between Cities**

Imagine you are a **railway planner** with **8 cities**, and you have a distance chart showing how far apart every pair of cities is (the "**pairwise distance matrix**"). You need to lay down the *shortest possible total track length* that still connects all cities into a single network.

**Naïve UPGMA Approach** (The "Compass-Walker"): A traveller who always walks exactly 1 km per hour, no matter the terrain. He assumes the time taken equals the distance, so he just connects the two closest cities first, then the next two closest, etc. The result is a tidy, balanced map — but it is **wrong** if some cities are hilly (fast-evolving) and some are flat (slow-evolving).

**Smart Neighbour-Joining Approach** (The "Surveyor"): A trained surveyor who first **corrects** the distance chart by subtracting the average distance to every other city. This correction removes the bias created by cities that are "lonely" or "distant" from everyone else. He then picks the pair of cities that are **closest neighbours after this correction**, joins them at a single "**internal node**", and recalculates the distances from this new hub to all remaining cities. The result is a more accurate, realistic map that **does not assume** everyone walks at the same speed.

> [!TIP]
> **The Core Intuition:** NJ uses a **"corrected distance"** called the **Q-matrix** to find the pair of operational taxonomic units (OTUs) that are *true* neighbours, hiding behind their respective distances to all other OTUs. It then joins them — hence the name **"Neighbour-Joining"**.

## 1.3 Why NJ is Important in Bioinformatics

| Application | Role of NJ |
|---|---|
| **Phylogenetic Tree Construction** | Gold standard for distance-based tree building |
| **Multiple Sequence Alignment refinement** | Used in tools like CLUSTALW, MAFFT |
| **Metagenomic classification** | Rapid OTU clustering in microbial ecology |
| **Viral evolution tracking** | SARS-CoV-2, Influenza lineage tracing |
| **Population genetics** | Haplotype network construction |

The NJ algorithm is **guaranteed to recover the correct tree** if the input distance matrix is **strictly additive** (i.e., the four-point condition holds for all quartets). This makes it a **statistically consistent estimator** under the minimum evolution criterion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Principles

The NJ algorithm rests on three pillars that every KTU answer must mention:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 1 — The Minimum Evolution (ME) Principle:** Among all possible unrooted trees connecting the $n$ taxa, the most plausible is the one with the **smallest total sum of branch lengths**.
>
> **Pillar 2 — The Neighbour Relation:** Two taxa $i$ and $j$ are *neighbours* on the true tree if there exists an **internal edge** that separates them from the rest of the tree without disrupting any other neighbour pair.
>
> **Pillar 3 — The Additivity Assumption:** For four taxa $a, b, c, d$ on an unrooted tree, the sum of the two *largest* of the three possible sums $d_{ab} + d_{cd}$, $d_{ac} + d_{bd}$, $d_{ad} + d_{bc}$ is **always greater than or equal to** the third sum (the **Four-Point Condition**).

## 2.2 The Q-Matrix — The Heart of the Algorithm

To find true neighbours (and not just the closest pair, which could be misled by long-branch attraction), NJ constructs a **corrected distance matrix**, the **Q-matrix**, defined element-wise as:

$$Q_{ij} = (n - 2) \cdot d_{ij} - u_i - u_j$$

where:
- $d_{ij}$ = observed pairwise distance between taxa $i$ and $j$
- $n$ = current number of OTUs (taxa) in the matrix
- $u_i = \sum_{k=1}^{n} d_{ik}$ = row sum of distances from taxon $i$ to every other taxon (including $i$, where $d_{ii} = 0$)

> [!NOTE]
> The row-sum $u_i$ acts as a **"loneliness penalty"** — taxa with large $u_i$ values (i.e., far from everyone else) are down-weighted, so that two long-branch taxa are not incorrectly identified as neighbours.

**The pair $(i, j)$ that minimises $Q_{ij}$** is selected as the next neighbour pair to be joined.

## 2.3 Branch Length Calculations

Once the neighbour pair $(i, j)$ is identified, a new internal node $k$ is created. The branch lengths from $k$ to $i$ and from $k$ to $j$ are calculated as:

$$L_{ki} = \frac{d_{ij}}{2} + \frac{u_i - u_j}{2(n - 2)}$$

$$L_{kj} = \frac{d_{ij}}{2} + \frac{u_j - u_i}{2(n - 2)}$$

The distance from the new node $k$ to any remaining taxon $m$ is:

$$d_{km} = \frac{d_{im} + d_{jm} - d_{ij}}{2}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Current OTU count | Number of active leaves |
| $d_{ij}$ | Given input distance | Pairwise evolutionary distance |
| $u_i$ | $\sum_{k} d_{ik}$ | Row sum (divergence of $i$) |
| $Q_{ij}$ | $(n-2)\,d_{ij} - u_i - u_j$ | Corrected neighbour score |
| $L_{ki}$ | $\frac{d_{ij}}{2} + \frac{u_i - u_j}{2(n-2)}$ | Branch length from new node to $i$ |
| $L_{kj}$ | $\frac{d_{ij}}{2} + \frac{u_j - u_i}{2(n-2)}$ | Branch length from new node to $j$ |
| $d_{km}$ | $\frac{d_{im} + d_{jm} - d_{ij}}{2}$ | New distance after joining |
| $S$ (ME) | $\sum_{\text{all edges } e} b_e$ | Total tree length to be minimised |
| $N_{\text{steps}}$ | $2n - 3$ | Number of joining steps for $n$ taxa (unrooted) |
| $S_{ij}$ | $\sum_{k \neq i,j} d_{ik} + d_{jk}$ | Sum of $i$ and $j$ row (excl. each other) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Q-matrix:** "**N D** minus **U U**" — $(n-2) \cdot d_{ij} - (u_i + u_j)$.

## 2.5 Algorithmic Logic Steps (Pseudocode)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The NJ Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic**

1. **Input:** An $n \times n$ symmetric distance matrix $D$ with $n \geq 3$.
2. **Compute row sums** $u_i$ for every taxon $i$.
3. **Compute the Q-matrix** using $Q_{ij} = (n-2)\,d_{ij} - u_i - u_j$.
4. **Select the pair $(i, j)$** that gives the **minimum** $Q_{ij}$ (most negative is best for $n \ge 4$; for $n=3$, just pick the smallest $Q_{ij}$).
5. **Join** $i$ and $j$ by creating a new internal node $k$.
6. **Compute branch lengths** $L_{ki}$ and $L_{kj}$ using the formulae above.
7. **Compute new distances** $d_{km}$ for all remaining taxa $m \neq i, j$.
8. **Reduce** the matrix from size $n$ to $n - 1$ (replace rows/columns $i$ and $j$ with the new row/column $k$).
9. **Repeat** Steps 2–8 until only **3 taxa** remain (these form the final unresolved star — the tree is then an unrooted quartet).
10. **Output:** The unrooted tree topology with branch lengths.

## 2.6 Engineering / Scientific Utility

In bioinformatics pipelines, NJ is the **workhorse** of the following tools:
- **CLUSTALW / CLUSTAL Omega** — first builds a guide tree using NJ for progressive MSA.
- **PHYLIP** (Felsenstein's package) — `neighbor` command.
- **MEGA** (Molecular Evolutionary Genetics Analysis) — default tree builder.
- **QuickTree** — fast NJ for large-scale 16S rRNA datasets.
- **PAUP\*** — used as a starting tree for maximum likelihood searches.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is NJ preferred in practice?**
> 1. **Speed:** $O(n^3)$ worst-case, but most real implementations are $O(n^2)$ per iteration → fast even for thousands of sequences.
> 2. **Robustness:** Does not assume a molecular clock.
> 3. **Statistical consistency:** Recovers the true tree under additive distances.
> 4. **Simplicity:** Closed-form formulae — no iterative optimisation needed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Q-Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Q-matrix the right "neighbour detector"?**

Consider an unrooted tree with $n$ leaves. For any two leaves $i$ and $j$, the sum of the lengths of *all* tree edges can be decomposed by "removing" the internal edge $e_{ij}$ that connects the two subtrees containing $i$ and $j$ respectively. Let the two subtrees have $n_i$ and $n_j$ leaves such that $n_i + n_j = n$.

The total tree length $S$ can be partitioned as:

$$S = e_{ij} + S_{i}^{*} + S_{j}^{*}$$

where $S_{i}^{*}$ and $S_{j}^{*}$ are the sums of branch lengths within subtrees $i$ and $j$.

**Key observation:** The sum of all pairwise distances $T = \sum_{a < b} d_{ab}$ satisfies:

$$T = (n - 1) \cdot S - n \cdot e_{ij} \cdot (some\ correction)$$

Following Saitou & Nei (1987), the minimum-evolution pair $(i,j)$ minimises a linear combination:

$$Q_{ij} = (n - 2) \cdot d_{ij} - \sum_{k} d_{ik} - \sum_{k} d_{jk}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Subtracting the row sums $u_i$ and $u_j$ cancels the contribution of all other distances. The result is a measure of "how much closer $i$ and $j$ are to each other *relative to* how far each is from the rest of the tree." If $i$ and $j$ are true neighbours, this value will be *most negative*.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Branch Length Formulae

Once $(i, j)$ is chosen, a new node $k$ is created. The new node $k$ has two edges to $i$ and $j$ of unknown lengths $L_{ki}$ and $L_{kj}$. For any third leaf $m$ not yet joined, the additivity of the tree gives:

$$d_{im} = L_{ki} + L_{kj} + d_{jm} - 2L_{kj} \quad \text{(this is wrong — let us re-derive carefully)}$$

### Correct Derivation

For an unrooted tree, the path from $i$ to $m$ passes through the internal node $k$:

$$d_{im} = L_{ki} + d_{km}$$

Similarly:

$$d_{jm} = L_{kj} + d_{km}$$

$$d_{ij} = L_{ki} + L_{kj}$$

From the first two equations:

$$d_{im} - d_{jm} = L_{ki} - L_{kj}$$

Combined with $L_{ki} + L_{kj} = d_{ij}$:

$$L_{ki} = \frac{d_{ij}}{2} + \frac{d_{im} - d_{jm}}{2} \quad (\text{elementary})$$

But $d_{im} - d_{jm}$ is not directly available in the reduced matrix at this stage. Using the row-sum correction (Saitou & Nei), one can show:

$$d_{im} - d_{jm} \approx \frac{u_i - u_j}{n - 2}$$

This yields the **canonical NJ branch-length formula**:

$$L_{ki} = \frac{d_{ij}}{2} + \frac{u_i - u_j}{2(n - 2)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** Memorise the symmetric form — the branch from the new node to $i$ is **half the original distance** plus a **"row-sum correction"** that biases the longer branch toward the more divergent taxon.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Four Taxa

Let us walk through the NJ algorithm on **$n = 4$** taxa: A, B, C, D.

### Step 1 — Input Distance Matrix

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 8 | 12 | 14 |
| **B** | 8 | 0 | 14 | 12 |
| **C** | 12 | 14 | 0 | 6 |
| **D** | 14 | 12 | 6 | 0 |

### Step 2 — Compute Row Sums

$$u_A = 0 + 8 + 12 + 14 = 34$$

$$u_B = 0 + 8 + 14 + 12 = 34$$

$$u_C = 0 + 12 + 14 + 6 = 32$$

$$u_D = 0 + 12 + 14 + 6 = 32$$

### Step 3 — Compute Q-Matrix

Using $Q_{ij} = (n-2) d_{ij} - u_i - u_j$ with $n=4$, so $n-2 = 2$:

$$Q_{AB} = 2(8) - 34 - 34 = 16 - 68 = -52$$

$$Q_{AC} = 2(12) - 34 - 32 = 24 - 66 = -42$$

$$Q_{AD} = 2(14) - 34 - 32 = 28 - 66 = -38$$

$$Q_{BC} = 2(14) - 34 - 32 = 28 - 66 = -38$$

$$Q_{BD} = 2(12) - 34 - 32 = 24 - 66 = -42$$

$$Q_{CD} = 2(6) - 32 - 32 = 12 - 64 = -52$$

### Step 4 — Select Minimum

Two pairs are tied: $(A, B)$ and $(C, D)$, both with $Q = -52$. Either is valid. Let us select $(C, D)$.

### Step 5 — Branch Lengths from New Node $k$ to C and D

$$L_{kC} = \frac{d_{CD}}{2} + \frac{u_C - u_D}{2(n-2)} = \frac{6}{2} + \frac{32 - 32}{4} = 3 + 0 = 3$$

$$L_{kD} = \frac{d_{CD}}{2} + \frac{u_D - u_C}{2(n-2)} = \frac{6}{2} + 0 = 3$$

### Step 6 — New Distances from $k$ to A and B

$$d_{kA} = \frac{d_{CA} + d_{DA} - d_{CD}}{2} = \frac{12 + 14 - 6}{2} = 10$$

$$d_{kB} = \frac{d_{CB} + d_{DB} - d_{CD}}{2} = \frac{14 + 12 - 6}{2} = 10$$

### Step 7 — Reduced Matrix (3 taxa)

| | A | B | k |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 8 | 10 |
| **B** | 8 | 0 | 10 |
| **k** | 10 | 10 | 0 |

### Step 8 — Final Join (A, B)

$$L_{AB'} = \frac{8}{2} + \frac{u_A' - u_B'}{2 \cdot 1} = 4 + 0 = 4$$

$$d_{k,\text{root}} = \frac{10 + 10 - 8}{2} = 6$$

### Final Tree

```
        A
         \___4
              \____6
        B ___/    \
         /         \____3
                    /    
                   k_____3
                          \
                           D
```

**Total tree length:** $S = 4 + 6 + 3 + 3 = \mathbf{16}$ units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of additivity:** The total $\sum d_{ij} = 8+12+14+14+12+6 = 66$. By the four-point condition, the tree is correct if $d_{AB} + d_{CD} = d_{AC} + d_{BD} = d_{AD} + d_{BC}$? Not exactly — the four-point condition says the *largest two* sums exceed the *smallest*. Here $d_{AB} + d_{CD} = 14$, $d_{AC} + d_{BD} = 24$, $d_{AD} + d_{BC} = 26$ → largest two are 24 and 26 (both > 14) ✓. Additivity holds.

## 3.4 Complexity Analysis

| Operation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| Single Q-matrix computation | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| One NJ iteration (find min, recompute) | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Full algorithm (n taxa → 3) | $O(n^3)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Practical (with heap-based min search) | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | $O(n)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For 1000 sequences:** Modern implementations (e.g., QuickTree) handle 10,000+ sequences in seconds — making NJ viable for large-scale 16S/18S microbiome studies.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — NJ Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Input n x n Distance Matrix D]) --> computeRows[Compute Row Sums u_i for all i]
    computeRows --> computeQ[Compute Q-Matrix: Q_ij = n-2 d_ij - u_i - u_j]
    computeQ --> findMin[Find pair i,j minimising Q_ij]
    findMin --> checkN{Is n = 3?}
    checkN -- No --> createNode[Create new internal node k]
    createNode --> branchLen[Compute L_ki and L_kj using branch-length formula]
    branchLen --> newDist[Compute new distances d_km for all m]
    newDist --> reduce[Reduce matrix from n x n to n-1 x n-1]
    reduce --> updateN[Set n = n - 1]
    updateN --> computeRows
    checkN -- Yes --> finalTree[Output final unrooted tree topology]
    finalTree --> stopZ([End])

    style startA fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style stopZ fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style computeQ fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style finalTree fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Architecture of NJ vs UPGMA

```mermaid
        N5[Reduce Matrix Iteratively]
    end

    D --> U1 --> U2 --> U3
    D --> N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> N4 --> N5

    U3 --> Compare{Final Use Case}

    Compare --> C1[Phylogeny with constant rates]
    Compare --> C2[Phylogeny with varying rates]

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Q-Matrix Module

```mermaid

        N[n - Number of OTUs]
    end

    end

    end

    end

    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Tree Reduction Across Iterations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Iteration 1: Join i and j to form node k1] --> S2[Iteration 2: Join k1 and m to form node k2]
    S2 --> S3[Iteration 3: Join k2 and p to form node k3]
    S3 --> S4[Iteration n-3: Final unresolved trifurcation]
    S4 --> OUT([Unrooted Phylogenetic Tree with n-3 Internal Nodes])

    style S1 fill:#aec7e8

```

> [!TIP]
> **Number of internal nodes in an unrooted binary tree of $n$ leaves:** $n - 2$ (this is why NJ stops at 3 leaves, since $3 - 2 = 1$ is the final unresolved internal node of the trifurcation).
