<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define bootstrapping in bioinformatics. Who introduced it to phylogenetics?
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (2 marks):** Bootstrapping is a non-parametric, computer-intensive statistical resampling technique used to estimate the sampling distribution of an estimator. In bioinformatics, it is most commonly used to assess the statistical reliability of branches in a phylogenetic tree by generating many pseudo-replicate alignments through **sampling with replacement** and rebuilding the tree for each replicate.
2. **Historical attribution (1 mark):** Introduced by **Efron (1979)** in general statistics, and adapted to phylogenetics by **Joseph Felsenstein (1985)** — published in the paper *"Confidence Limits on Phylogenies: An Approach Using the Bootstrap"* in *Evolution*.

---

### Q2. What is a consensus tree? Differentiate between majority-rule and strict consensus trees.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **consensus tree** (1 mark) summarizes a set of bootstrap trees by retaining only clades that appear with sufficient frequency.
- **Majority-rule consensus** (1 mark): Contains every clade that occurs in $\geq$ 50% of the input trees.
- **Strict consensus** (1 mark): Contains only those clades that occur in **100%** of the input trees — far more conservative; the resulting tree is often less resolved.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse bootstrap with jackknife.** Jackknife leaves out one column at a time; bootstrap samples **with replacement**.
> - **Always specify "with replacement"** — losing this phrase costs 1–2 marks.
> - **Do not write "bootstrap of the tree"** — the bootstrap is of the **aligned columns**, not the tree itself.
> - **State the confidence threshold you are using (70%)** explicitly when interpreting a bootstrap value, or the examiner will deduct marks.
> - **Mathematical derivations must show the formula first, then substitute the values** — this is the KTU valuation key pattern.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Modular Long-Answer Choice

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**
Explain the bootstrap method for assessing phylogenetic tree confidence. Describe the algorithm in detail and state the formula for computing a bootstrap value. Why is "sampling with replacement" essential? **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**
In a study, B = 500 bootstrap replicates were generated for a clade D grouping taxa P, Q, R, S. The clade appeared in 365 of the 500 trees. (i) Calculate the bootstrap value of clade D. (ii) Comment on its statistical support using the standard KTU threshold. (iii) Compute the approximate 95% confidence interval for the bootstrap proportion.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Background (1 mark):** A phylogenetic tree is a point estimate. We need a way to quantify the uncertainty associated with each branch. Classical standard errors are not analytically tractable for tree estimators, so we use a **resampling** approach.

2. **Algorithm (4 marks — one mark per major step):**
   - **Step 1:** Take the multiple sequence alignment $\mathbf{X}$ with $n$ columns.
   - **Step 2:** For each of $B$ replicates ($B = 100, 500, 1000, 10000$):
       - Resample $n$ columns **with replacement** from $\mathbf{X}$ to form $\mathbf{X}^{*}_b$.
       - Build a tree $T_b$ from $\mathbf{X}^{*}_b$ using the same inference method (NJ/MP/ML).
   - **Step 3:** For every clade $c$ in the reference tree $T_0$, count how many of the $T_b$ contain $c$. Call this count $B_c$.
   - **Step 4:** Compute the bootstrap value using the formula given below.

3. **Formula (1 mark):**
$$BV_c = \frac{B_c}{B} \times 100\%$$

4. **Why "with replacement" (1 mark):** Sampling with replacement is essential because:
   - It makes each draw **independent** of the others.
   - It generates **variability** between pseudo-replicates (some columns repeated, others absent).
   - The expected fraction of unique columns is $1 - 1/e \approx 63.2\%$, ensuring each replicate is **similar but not identical** to $\mathbf{X}$.
   - Without replacement we would simply permute the columns, which is the **jackknife**, not the bootstrap.

**Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Bootstrap value (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BV_D &= \frac{B_D}{B} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{365}{500} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{73}{100} \times 100\% \\
&= 73\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Interpretation (2 marks):**

Since $BV_D = 73\% \ge 70\%$ (the KTU standard threshold), clade D is **well supported** and the grouping of taxa P, Q, R, S is considered **statistically reliable** at the conventional confidence level.

**(iii) 95% Confidence Interval (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{p} &= \frac{365}{500} = 0.73 \\
\mathrm{SE}(\hat{p}) &= \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{B}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{0.73 \times 0.27}{500}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{0.1971}{500}} \\
&= \sqrt{3.942 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&\approx 0.01985
\end{aligned}
$$

95% CI $\approx \hat{p} \pm 1.96 \times \mathrm{SE}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CI} &\approx 0.73 \pm 1.96 \times 0.01985 \\
&\approx 0.73 \pm 0.0389 \\
&\approx [0.6911, \; 0.7689] \\
&\approx [69.11\%, \; 76.89\%]
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the lower bound (69.11%) is just below 70%, the support is **borderline**; most statisticians would still consider it adequate, but a cautious interpretation is warranted.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Modular Choice

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**
Compare and contrast the **bootstrap** and **jackknife** resampling techniques. State one advantage and one limitation of each in the context of phylogenetic analysis. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**
A research group uses 200 bootstrap replicates for an initial study and obtains a bootstrap value of 75% for a clade. They repeat the analysis with 1000 replicates and obtain 71% for the same clade. (i) Explain why the two values differ. (ii) Which value is more reliable and why? (iii) What is the approximate standard error in each case?

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Feature | Bootstrap | Jackknife |
|---|---|---|
| **Resampling scheme** (1 mark) | Sampling **with replacement** | Leaving out **one observation at a time** |
| **Number of replicates** (1 mark) | Arbitrary, typically $B = 100\text{--}10000$ | Fixed at $n$ (one per column) |
| **Variance estimation** (1 mark) | Direct from pseudo-replicate distribution | Analytic formulas from leave-one-out estimates |
| **Use in phylogenetics** (1 mark) | Standard for branch support | Less common; mainly for outlier detection |
| **Advantage** (1 mark) | More flexible, robust for non-linear estimators (e.g., trees) | Deterministic, computationally cheap, gives quick sanity check |
| **Limitation** (1 mark) | Computationally expensive for ML; assumes observed data is representative | Less stable for complex estimators; only $n$ replicates |
| **Reliability of confidence intervals** (1 mark) | Generally more accurate for non-smooth statistics | Tends to underestimate variance for non-linear estimators |

**Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Why the two values differ (2 marks):**

Both estimates (75% and 71%) are samples from the **same underlying true probability** $p$ that the clade appears. The observed proportion $\hat{p} = B_c / B$ has standard error proportional to $1/\sqrt{B}$. With $B = 200$, the SE is roughly $\sqrt{5}$ times larger than with $B = 1000$. The discrepancy of 4 percentage points falls well within this expected sampling fluctuation, and is therefore **not contradictory**.

**(ii) More reliable value (2 marks):**

The estimate from $B = 1000$ replicates (**71%**) is **more reliable**, because its standard error is smaller. As $B$ increases, $\hat{p}$ converges to the true bootstrap probability $p$ by the **Law of Large Numbers**.

**(iii) Standard error in each case (3 marks):**

For $B = 200$ with $\hat{p} = 0.75$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{SE}_{200} &= \sqrt{\frac{0.75 \times 0.25}{200}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{0.1875}{200}} \\
&= \sqrt{9.375 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&\approx 0.0306 \;\;(\text{or } 3.06\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $B = 1000$ with $\hat{p} = 0.71$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{SE}_{1000} &= \sqrt{\frac{0.71 \times 0.29}{1000}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{0.2059}{1000}} \\
&= \sqrt{2.059 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&\approx 0.01435 \;\;(\text{or } 1.44\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (extra mark-worthy):** Increasing replicates from 200 to 1000 reduced the standard error by a factor of $\sqrt{1000/200} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$, exactly as predicted by the $1/\sqrt{B}$ scaling.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Bootstrapping = resampling with replacement** (not jackknife).
- **Originator in phylogenetics:** Joseph Felsenstein, 1985 (*Evolution*).
- **General statistical originator:** Bradley Efron, 1979.
- **Core formula to memorize:** $BV_c = (B_c / B) \times 100\%$.
- **Pseudo-replicate size** = original alignment length $n$.
- **Standard replicate counts:** $B = 100, 500, 1000, 10000$.
- **Confidence threshold (KTU convention):** $\geq 70\%$ = well supported; some use 50% or 80%.
- **Unique columns in a pseudo-replicate:** $\approx 63.2\% = 1 - 1/e$.
- **Standard error of bootstrap proportion:** $\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/B}$.
- **95% CI of bootstrap proportion:** $\hat{p} \pm 1.96 \times \mathrm{SE}$.
- **Two types of consensus tree:** majority-rule ($\geq 50\%$) and strict (100%).
- **Software tools:** PHYLIP (seqboot + consensus), RAxML, IQ-TREE, MEGA, PAUP*.
- **Always specify the inference method used** (NJ, MP, ML) when reporting bootstrap values — the same data can yield very different trees under different methods.
- **High BV does not mean the tree is biologically correct** — it only means the data strongly support that branching pattern; model mis-specification can still mislead.
- **Use raw text, not the `|` character, in markdown tables for absolute values** to avoid breaking the table layout (KCU-friendly formatting rule).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combinatorial Pattern Matching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST743 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Combinatorial Pattern Matching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Combinatorial Pattern Matching (CPM)** is a foundational sub-discipline of string algorithms (also known as algorithmic stringology) that deals with finding occurrences of one or more string structures (called *patterns*) within a larger string or text corpus, using discrete mathematical and combinatorial tools. In the context of **bioinformatics**, CPM refers to the algorithmic identification of biologically meaningful motifs, conserved subsequences, regulatory elements, repeated regions, and sequence signatures within genomic DNA, RNA, and protein sequences.

Formally, given a **text** $T = T[1 \ldots n]$ of length $n$ over a finite alphabet $\Sigma$ and a **pattern** $P = P[1 \ldots m]$ of length $m$ (where typically $m \leq n$), the combinatorial pattern matching problem seeks to find all valid shifts $s \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n - m + 1\}$ such that:

$$T[s \ldots s + m - 1] = P[1 \ldots m]$$

This is known as the **Exact Pattern Matching** problem. More general variants include **Approximate Pattern Matching** (allowing mismatches or gaps, modeled via edit distance), **Regular Expression Matching**, and **Parameterized Matching**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Combinatorial Pattern Matching forms the algorithmic backbone of sequence alignment, motif discovery, and genome assembly. The student must master both **naive approaches** for conceptual clarity and **efficient algorithms** (KMP, Boyer-Moore, Suffix Automata) for analytical performance.

## 1.2 Alphabet Specificity in Bioinformatics

In computational biology, the alphabet $\Sigma$ is determined by the biomolecule under study:

| Biomolecule | Alphabet $\Sigma$ | Cardinality $\vert \Sigma \vert$ |
|---|---|---|
| DNA (nucleic acid) | $\{A, C, G, T\}$ | **4** |
| RNA | $\{A, C, G, U\}$ | **4** |
| Proteins (amino acids) | 20 standard residues | **20** |
| Extended genetic code | $\{A, C, G, T, N\}$ (N = ambiguous) | **5** |

The cardinality of $\Sigma$ directly impacts algorithmic efficiency because a smaller alphabet increases the likelihood of random pattern matches, demanding more rigorous statistical evaluation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Needle in a Genomic Haystack"

Imagine a librarian tasked with finding every occurrence of the phrase **"the quick brown fox"** inside a 1,000-page manuscript, but the phrase might be misspelled, partially written, or wrapped across line breaks. The librarian cannot read the entire book character by character for every possible starting position — that would take a lifetime. 

**Combinatorial Pattern Matching** is the digital equivalent of giving the librarian a smart magnifying glass that:
- **Skips obviously irrelevant sections** (like the *Boyer-Moore bad-character heuristic*).
- **Remembers which prefixes already matched** to avoid redundant comparisons (like the *KMP failure function*).
- **Pre-indexes the entire book** for future rapid queries (like *Suffix Trees* and *Suffix Arrays*).

In bioinformatics, the "manuscript" is a **chromosome** (often 250 million base-pairs long in human DNA), and the "phrases" are genes, promoters, transcription factor binding sites, or evolutionary conserved motifs.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Pattern Matching Problems

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Classification (Most Common Variants Encountered in KTU Examinations):**
>
> 1. **Exact String Matching** — Hamming distance = 0 (no mismatches allowed).
> 2. **Approximate String Matching** — Edit distance (Levenshtein) $\leq k$ for a given threshold $k$.
> 3. **Multiple Pattern Matching** — Simultaneous search for a set of patterns $\{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_r\}$ (solved via *Aho-Corasick* algorithm).
> 4. **Regular Expression Matching** — Patterns defined by a formal regular language.
> 5. **Parameterized Matching** — Pattern matches up to a bijective renaming of symbols (used in software plagiarism detection and motif finding).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Naïve (Brute-Force) String Matcher

The naïve algorithm slides the pattern $P$ across the text $T$, comparing character by character at each alignment. At alignment position $s$, it proceeds through indices $i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$. Upon the first mismatch at $i$, it abandons the current alignment and shifts to $s + 1$.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic Steps

1. Initialize shift counter $s \leftarrow 0$.
2. For each alignment $s \in [0, n - m]$:
   - Set $i \leftarrow 0$.
   - While $i < m$ and $T[s + i] = P[i]$, increment $i$.
   - If $i = m$, report $s$ as a valid match (pattern occurs at position $s$).
   - Else, shift to $s \leftarrow s + 1$.
3. Terminate when $s > n - m$.

### 2.1.2 Why the Naïve Method Fails Biologically

The naïve method performs $O(n \cdot m)$ character comparisons in the worst case (e.g., pattern $P = a^{m-1}b$ against text $T = a^{n-1}b$ yields $O(nm)$). For the human genome where $n \approx 3 \times 10^9$ base pairs, this becomes computationally infeasible without preprocessing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** The naïve method is **not** rejected outright in biological applications. It serves as the *baseline* against which sophisticated algorithms (KMP, BM) are benchmarked. Many modern bioinformatics tools (e.g., **BLAST**) actually employ a **seed-and-extend** heuristic that is conceptually an optimized version of naïve matching with skipping rules.

## 2.2 The Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) Algorithm

The **KMP algorithm**, proposed by Donald Knuth, Vaughan Pratt, and James H. Morris in 1977, achieves $O(n + m)$ worst-case time complexity by eliminating redundant comparisons using a preprocessing phase on the pattern alone.

### 2.2.1 The Failure Function (Prefix Function) $\pi$

The failure function $\pi[i]$ for $i \in [1, m]$ is defined as the length of the **longest proper prefix of $P[1 \ldots i]$** that is also a **suffix of $P[1 \ldots i]$**. Formally:

$$\pi[i] = \max\{k : 0 \leq k < i \text{ and } P[1 \ldots k] = P[i - k + 1 \ldots i]\}$$

with $\pi[1] = 0$ by convention (the empty string is the only proper prefix of a single character).

### 2.2.2 Why KMP Works — The "How" Behind the Magic

When a mismatch occurs at text position $j$ and pattern position $i$, the naïve algorithm discards all information about the matched prefix $P[1 \ldots i - 1]$. KMP exploits the internal structure of this prefix: if a suffix of $P[1 \ldots i - 1]$ matches a prefix of $P$, the algorithm can shift $P$ to align these matching portions, bypassing already-verified characters.

## 2.3 The Boyer-Moore (BM) Algorithm

The **Boyer-Moore algorithm** (1977) is a **sub-linear** matcher in the average case — it often inspects fewer than $n$ characters of the text. It achieves this by aligning the pattern at the rightmost end and scanning **right-to-left**, applying two heuristics upon mismatch:

1. **Bad Character Heuristic** — Shift $P$ so that the rightmost occurrence of the mismatched text character in $P$ aligns with it.
2. **Good Suffix Heuristic** — Shift $P$ so that the already-matched suffix re-aligns with another occurrence of itself in $P$.

The actual shift is the **maximum** of these two heuristic shifts.

## 2.4 Suffix Trees and Suffix Arrays

### 2.4.1 Suffix Tree

A **suffix tree** $ST(T)$ of a text $T$ of length $n$ is a compact trie (prefix tree) containing all $n$ suffixes of $T$. It has $O(n)$ nodes and $O(n)$ edges, and can be constructed in $O(n)$ time using **Ukkonen's algorithm** (1995).

A pattern $P$ of length $m$ can be searched in $ST(T)$ in $O(m)$ time — **independent of $n$**. This makes suffix trees the gold standard for **single-text, many-pattern** queries.

### 2.4.2 Suffix Array

A **suffix array** $SA(T)$ is a sorted array of all $n$ starting positions of the suffixes of $T$, ordered lexicographically. It is a **space-efficient** alternative to the suffix tree, requiring only $O(n \log n)$ bits (versus $O(n \log \sigma)$ bits for suffix trees where $\sigma = \vert \Sigma \vert$).

Pattern matching using a suffix array proceeds via **binary search** in $O(m \log n)$ time per query.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Reference

| Algorithm | Preprocessing Time | Matching Time | Space | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Naïve Brute-Force | $O(1)$ | $O(n \cdot m)$ | $O(1)$ | Short patterns, baseline benchmarks |
| **KMP** | $O(m)$ | $O(n + m)$ | $O(m)$ | Worst-case guarantees, online streaming |
| **Boyer-Moore** | $O(m + \sigma)$ | $\leq O(n)$, avg. $O(n/m)$ | $O(m + \sigma)$ | Long patterns, large alphabets (English text, protein DBs) |
| **Aho-Corasick** | $O(M)$ where $M = \sum \vert P_i \vert$ | $O(n + \text{occurrences})$ | $O(M)$ | Multiple pattern matching (motif libraries) |
| **Suffix Tree** | $O(n)$ (Ukkonen) | $O(m + \text{occurrences})$ | $O(n)$ | Many queries on static text (genome indexing) |
| **Suffix Array** | $O(n \log n)$ or $O(n)$ (SA-IS, DC) | $O(m \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Space-constrained indexing, read mapping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Note on Formula Usage:** The complexity bounds above assume $\vert \Sigma \vert = \sigma$ and $\vert T \vert = n$, $\vert P \vert = m$. Examiners frequently test the *transitions* between these complexities in part (b) of 14-mark questions.

## 2.6 Engineering and Biological Utility

- **Genome Assembly** — Suffix trees/arrays underpin overlap-layout-consensus (OLC) assemblers.
- **Read Mapping** — BWA, Bowtie use **Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWT)** + suffix arrays for $O(m)$ short-read alignment.
- **Motif Discovery** — TRANSFAC, JASPAR databases are queried via exact/approximate CPM.
- **Primer Design** — PCR primer uniqueness is verified by string matching against the full genome.
- **CRISPR Off-Target Detection** — Guide RNA sequences are matched against the entire reference genome using efficient CPM algorithms.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — KMP Failure Function Construction

Let us consider the pattern $P = \text{"ACACAGT"}$ with $m = 7$. We will compute the prefix function $\pi[i]$ for each $i \in [1, 7]$.

### Step 1: Initialize the array $\pi[1 \ldots 7]$

By definition, $\pi[1] = 0$ because a string of length 1 has no proper prefix that is also a suffix.

### Step 2: Compute $\pi[2]$ for $P[1 \ldots 2] = \text{"AC"}$

Proper prefixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{"A"}\}$.
Proper suffixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{"C"}\}$.
Intersection: $\{\varepsilon\}$, with maximum length $0$.
Therefore $\pi[2] = 0$.

### Step 3: Compute $\pi[3]$ for $P[1 \ldots 3] = \text{"AC\textquotedbl A"}$

Proper prefixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{"A"}, \text{"AC"}\}$.
Proper suffixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{"CA"}, \text{"A"}\}$.
Intersection: $\{\varepsilon, \text{"A"}\}$, with maximum length $1$.
Therefore $\pi[3] = 1$.

### Step 4: Compute $\pi[4]$ for $P[1 \ldots 4] = \text{"ACAC"}$

Proper prefixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{"A"}, \text{"AC"}, \text{"ACA"}\}$.
Proper suffixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{"CAC"}, \text{"AC"}, \text{"C"}\}$.
Intersection: $\{\varepsilon, \text{"AC"}\}$, with maximum length $2$.
Therefore $\pi[4] = 2$.

### Step 5: Compute $\pi[5]$ for $P[1 \ldots 5] = \text{"ACACA"}$

Proper prefixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{"A"}, \text{"AC"}, \text{"ACA"}, \text{"ACAC"}\}$.
Proper suffixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{"CACA"}, \text{"ACA"}, \text{"CA"}, \text{"A"}\}$.
Intersection: $\{\varepsilon, \text{"A"}, \text{"ACA"}\}$, with maximum length $3$.
Therefore $\pi[5] = 3$.

### Step 6: Compute $\pi[6]$ for $P[1 \ldots 6] = \text{"ACACAG"}$

We try to extend $\pi[5] = 3$. The candidate is $P[4] = \text{"A"}$ matching $P[6] = \text{"G"}$ — **mismatch**. Fall back to $\pi[3] = 1$. Try $P[2] = \text{"C"}$ matching $P[6] = \text{"G"}$ — **mismatch**. Fall back to $\pi[1] = 0$. No match, so $\pi[6] = 0$.

### Step 7: Compute $\pi[7]$ for $P[1 \ldots 7] = \text{"ACACAGT"}$

Extend $\pi[6] = 0$. Try $P[1] = \text{"A"}$ matching $P[7] = \text{"T"}$ — **mismatch**. Fall back to $\pi[0] = 0$ (convention). Therefore $\pi[7] = 0$.

### Final Failure Function Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi &= [\;\pi[1], \pi[2], \pi[3], \pi[4], \pi[5], \pi[6], \pi[7]\;] \\
&= [\;\;0,\;\;\;0,\;\;\;1,\;\;\;2,\;\;\;3,\;\;\;0,\;\;\;0\;\;]
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — KMP Search on Genomic Text

Let the text $T = \text{"ACATCACACAGT"}$ and pattern $P = \text{"ACACAGT"}$. We will execute the KMP search.

### Initialization
- $q \leftarrow 0$ (number of characters matched so far).
- $i$ iterates over text positions from 2 to $n = 13$.

### Step-by-Step Trace

| Step $i$ | $T[i]$ | $P[q+1]$ | Match? | Action | New $q$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | C | A (since q=0) | ✗ | $q \leftarrow \pi[0] = 0$ (still 0) | 0 |
| 3 | A | A | ✓ | $q \leftarrow 1$ | 1 |
| 4 | T | C | ✗ | $q \leftarrow \pi[1] = 0$ | 0 |
| 5 | C | A | ✗ | $q \leftarrow 0$ | 0 |
| 6 | A | A | ✓ | $q \leftarrow 1$ | 1 |
| 7 | C | C | ✓ | $q \leftarrow 2$ | 2 |
| 8 | A | A | ✓ | $q \leftarrow 3$ | 3 |
| 9 | C | C | ✓ | $q \leftarrow 4$ | 4 |
| 10 | A | A | ✓ | $q \leftarrow 5$ | 5 |
| 11 | G | G | ✓ | $q \leftarrow 6$ | 6 |
| 12 | T | T | ✓ | $q \leftarrow 7 = m$ | **MATCH at position $i - m = 12 - 7 = 5$** |

### Result
The pattern $P = \text{"ACACAGT"}$ occurs in $T$ starting at position **5** (1-indexed). This corresponds to a **TATA-box-like** or **promoter-like** motif in molecular biology contexts.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Boyer-Moore Bad Character Rule

Let $P = \text{"GCAGAG"}$, $T = \text{"GGCGCATAGCAGAG"}$.

### Step 1: Preprocess the Last-Occurrence Function
For each character $x \in \Sigma$ (here, $\{A, C, G, T\}$), we record the rightmost position of $x$ in $P$:

| Character $x$ | Last position $\lambda(x)$ |
|---|---|
| A | 4 |
| C | 3 |
| G | 6 |
| T | 0 (not present, default shift = pattern length) |

### Step 2: Search
- Align $P$ at position 1. Compare right-to-left: $P[6]=\text{G} = T[6]=\text{G}$ ✓, $P[5]=\text{A} = T[5]=\text{A}$ ✓, $P[4]=\text{G} \neq T[4]=\text{C}$ ✗.
- Bad character is $T[4] = \text{C}$. Last occurrence of C in $P$ is at position 3.
- New alignment: $s \leftarrow s + (6 - 3) = 1 + 3 = 4$.
- Align $P$ at position 4. Continue right-to-left comparisons.

### Result
The algorithm successfully finds the match at position 8, having inspected only a fraction of the text characters — confirming its **sub-linear** average behavior.

## 3.4 Analytical Complexity Derivation for KMP

The total number of character comparisons in KMP is bounded by $2n$, which establishes the $O(n)$ bound:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Comparisons}_{\text{KMP}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[\text{comparison at step } i] \\
&\leq 2n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Proof Sketch (for advanced students):**
- Let $q_i$ be the value of $q$ after processing text position $i$.
- Each successful comparison increments $q$ by 1, contributing $\leq n$ increments total.
- Each failed comparison decrements $q$ via the failure function (which is non-negative), and since $q$ was previously incremented, the decrements are bounded by the total increments.
- Therefore, total comparisons $\leq 2n$.

## 3.5 Summary Matrix — Algorithmic Trade-offs

| Property | Naïve | KMP | Boyer-Moore | Suffix Tree | Suffix Array |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Time (Search)** | $O(nm)$ | $O(n)$ | $\leq O(n)$, avg $O(n/m)$ | $O(m)$ per query | $O(m \log n)$ per query |
| **Time (Build)** | — | $O(m)$ | $O(m + \sigma)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ or $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Space** | $O(1)$ | $O(m)$ | $O(m + \sigma)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Online?** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (needs full pattern) | ✗ | ✗ |
| **DNA Suitability** | Low | High | Very High | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Multiple Patterns** | ✗ | ✗ (extend via Aho-Corasick) | ✗ (extend via Commentz-Walter) | ✓ | ✓ |

For sequence alignment and motif discovery, the **Suffix Array (SA)** is paired with the **Longest Common Prefix (LCP)** array, forming a complete indexing structure.
