# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Combinatorial Pattern Matching problem. Distinguish between exact and approximate string matching with a bioinformatics example.**

**Model Answer:**
Combinatorial Pattern Matching is the algorithmic problem of finding occurrences of a pattern $P$ of length $m$ within a text $T$ of length $n$ over an alphabet $\Sigma$. In **exact matching**, we require $T[s \ldots s + m - 1] = P[1 \ldots m]$ (Hamming distance = 0). In **approximate matching**, we allow a bounded number $k$ of mismatches, insertions, or deletions such that the edit distance $d(T[s \ldots], P) \leq k$.

*Bioinformatics Example:* Searching for the conserved motif $P = \text{"TATAAA"}$ (TATA box) in a genomic promoter region is exact matching. Searching for a transcription factor binding site that may tolerate 1–2 SNPs (single nucleotide polymorphisms) is approximate matching with $k = 2$.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the prefix (failure) function in the Knuth-Morris-Pratt algorithm? Why is it called a "failure" function?**

**Model Answer:**
The prefix function $\pi[i]$ returns the length of the longest proper prefix of $P[1 \ldots i]$ that is also a suffix of $P[1 \ldots i]$. It is called a "failure" function because, upon a character mismatch at position $i$, the algorithm consults $\pi[i - 1]$ to determine how far the pattern pointer can fall back without losing any valid alignment — effectively, it records *how to recover from a failure*. This preprocessing step eliminates the redundant re-comparisons that plague the naïve algorithm.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) algorithm in detail. Construct the failure function $\pi$ for the pattern $P = \text{"ABABACA"}$ and trace the KMP matching procedure on the text $T = \text{"ABACABABABACA"}$. Mention the time and space complexity. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the Aho-Corasick algorithm for multiple pattern matching. How is it relevant to genome-wide motif discovery? Illustrate with a suitable diagram. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1: Definition Recap**
The KMP algorithm achieves $O(n + m)$ worst-case time by using the prefix function $\pi$ to avoid re-examining text characters.

**Step 2: Failure Function Construction for $P = \text{"ABABACA"}$**

We compute $\pi[i]$ for $i = 1, \ldots, 7$:

- $i = 1$: $\pi[1] = 0$ (by convention).
- $i = 2$: $P[1 \ldots 2] = \text{"AB"}$. Proper prefixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{A}\}$. Proper suffixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{B}\}$. Intersection: $\{\varepsilon\}$. $\pi[2] = 0$.
- $i = 3$: $P[1 \ldots 3] = \text{"ABA"}$. Proper prefixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{A}, \text{AB}\}$. Proper suffixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{BA}, \text{A}\}$. Intersection: $\{\varepsilon, \text{A}\}$. $\pi[3] = 1$.
- $i = 4$: $P[1 \ldots 4] = \text{"ABAB"}$. Proper prefixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{A}, \text{AB}, \text{ABA}\}$. Proper suffixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{BAB}, \text{AB}, \text{B}\}$. Intersection: $\{\varepsilon, \text{AB}\}$. $\pi[4] = 2$.
- $i = 5$: $P[1 \ldots 5] = \text{"ABABA"}$. Proper prefixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{A}, \text{AB}, \text{ABA}, \text{ABAB}\}$. Proper suffixes: $\{\varepsilon, \text{BABA}, \text{ABA}, \text{BA}, \text{A}\}$. Intersection: $\{\varepsilon, \text{A}, \text{ABA}\}$. $\pi[5] = 3$.
- $i = 6$: Try to extend $\pi[5] = 3$. Check $P[4] = \text{B}$ against $P[6] = \text{C}$: **mismatch**. Fall back to $\pi[3] = 1$. Check $P[2] = \text{B}$ against $P[6] = \text{C}$: **mismatch**. Fall back to $\pi[1] = 0$. $\pi[6] = 0$.
- $i = 7$: Extend $\pi[6] = 0$. Check $P[1] = \text{A}$ against $P[7] = \text{A}$: **match**. $\pi[7] = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi = [0,\; 0,\; 1,\; 2,\; 3,\; 0,\; 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: KMP Search on $T = \text{"ABACABABABACA"}$**

| $i$ | $T[i]$ | $P[q+1]$ | Action | New $q$ | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | A | Match | 1 | — |
| 2 | B | B | Match | 2 | — |
| 3 | A | A | Match | 3 | — |
| 4 | C | B | Mismatch; $q \leftarrow \pi[2]=0$ | 0 | — |
| 5 | A | A | Match | 1 | — |
| 6 | B | B | Match | 2 | — |
| 7 | A | A | Match | 3 | — |
| 8 | B | B | Match | 4 | — |
| 9 | A | A | Match | 5 | — |
| 10 | B | C | Mismatch; $q \leftarrow \pi[5]=3$ | 3 | — |
| 11 | A | B | Mismatch; $q \leftarrow \pi[3]=1$ | 1 | — |
| 12 | C | B | Mismatch; $q \leftarrow \pi[1]=0$ | 0 | — |
| 13 | A | A | Match | 1 | — |

Result: The match is **not found** in the given text, demonstrating KMP's correctness in $O(n + m) = O(20)$ comparisons.

**Step 4: Complexity Statement**
- Preprocessing: $O(m)$ time, $O(m)$ space.
- Matching: $O(n)$ time, no additional space required.
- Total: $O(n + m)$ time, $O(m)$ auxiliary space.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Aho-Corasick Algorithm Overview:**
The Aho-Corasick (AC) algorithm, proposed in 1975, extends the KMP framework to simultaneously search for a **set of $r$ patterns** $\{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_r\}$ in a text $T$ in $O(n + M + \text{occurrences})$ time, where $M = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \vert P_i \vert$.

**Construction Steps:**

1. **Trie Construction:** Build a trie (prefix tree) of all patterns. Each node represents a prefix of some pattern.
2. **Failure Function (Goto Failure Links):** For each node, compute the longest proper suffix of the string represented by that node that is also a prefix of *some* pattern in the trie (analogous to KMP's $\pi$, but at the trie level).
3. **Output Function (Dictionary Links):** Mark nodes that correspond to the end of a pattern and propagate this information via the failure links.

**Searching:** Traverse the text character by character, following the trie edges and using failure links upon mismatches. Whenever an output node is reached, report all patterns ending at the current position.

**Relevance to Genome-Wide Motif Discovery:**

In genome-wide motif discovery, researchers seek instances of a library of transcription factor binding motifs (e.g., from JASPAR or TRANSFAC) across the entire genome. A naïve approach would run KMP $r$ times — yielding $O(rn)$ time. Aho-Corasick reduces this to a **single pass** over the genome, achieving $O(n + M + \text{hits})$ time. This is critical for:
- **ChIP-seq peak annotation** — finding enriched motifs in immunoprecipitated DNA.
- **Promoter analysis** — identifying clusters of regulatory elements.
- **Virus-host interaction studies** — detecting integration motifs in viral genomes.

**Diagram (AC Automaton for $P_1 = \text{"AT"}, P_2 = \text{"ATC"}, P_3 = \text{"TCA"}$):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Root"] --> nodeA["A"]
    rootA --> nodeT["T"]
    nodeA --> nodeAT["T - Output: AT"]
    nodeAT --> nodeATC["C - Output: ATC"]
    nodeT --> nodeTC["C"]
    nodeTC --> nodeTCA["A - Output: TCA"]
```

The failure link from `nodeAT` points back to `nodeT` (since "T" is the longest proper suffix of "AT" that is also a trie prefix). This enables seamless transition upon a mismatch.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the construction of a suffix tree for a biological sequence. How is pattern matching performed using a suffix tree? Discuss the time complexity. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWT) and its role in modern short-read aligners like Bowtie and BWA. Why is BWT considered the "compressed" form of a suffix array? **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Suffix Tree Definition:**
A suffix tree of a string $T$ of length $n$ is a rooted, directed tree with the following properties:
- Exactly $n$ leaf nodes, numbered 1 to $n$ (each corresponding to a suffix).
- Every internal node (except the root) has at least two children.
- Each edge is labeled with a non-empty substring of $T$.
- No two edges from the same node begin with the same character.
- Concatenation of edge labels from root to leaf $i$ yields the $i$-th suffix of $T$.

**Example Construction for $T = \text{"TATAT$"}$ (where $ is the unique sentinel):**

The suffixes are:
1. `TATAT$`
2. `ATAT$`
3. `TAT$`
4. `AT$`
5. `T$`
6. `$`

Sorted lexicographically:
1. `$`
2. `AT$`
3. `ATAT$`
4. `T$`
5. `TAT$`
6. `TATAT$`

After path compression (merging single-child chains):

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Root"] --> nodeDollar["$ (leaf 1)"]
    rootA --> nodeA["A"]
    nodeA --> nodeT["T"]
    nodeT --> nodeDollar2["$ (leaf 2: AT$)"]
    nodeT --> nodeAT["AT$ (leaf 3: ATAT$)"]
    rootA --> nodeT2["T"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeDollar3["$ (leaf 4: T$)"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeAT2["AT$ (leaf 5: TAT$)"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeATAT2["ATAT$ (leaf 6: TATAT$)"]
```

**Pattern Matching Using Suffix Tree:**
To search for a pattern $P$ of length $m$ in $T$:
1. Start at the root.
2. Follow the edge whose label begins with $P[1]$.
3. If the edge label is a substring of length $\ell$, compare the next $\min(\ell, m - k)$ characters of $P$ with the edge label, where $k$ is the number of $P$ characters already matched.
4. If a mismatch occurs or no such edge exists, report "no match."
5. If $P$ is fully matched before reaching a leaf, report all leaves in the subtree as matching positions.

**Time Complexity:**
- Construction: $O(n)$ using **Ukkonen's online algorithm** (1995).
- Pattern search: $O(m)$ per query — **independent of $n$**.
- Space: $O(n)$.

**Biological Significance:**
Suffix trees enable rapid identification of all occurrences of a gene, regulatory element, or restriction site in a chromosome. They also form the basis for finding the **longest common substring** between two sequences in $O(n_1 + n_2)$ time via the **generalized suffix tree**.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWT):**
The BWT of a text $T$ is a reversible permutation that groups together characters with similar "left contexts." It is constructed by:
1. Forming the $n$ cyclic rotations of $T$.
2. Sorting these rotations lexicographically.
3. Taking the **last column** of the sorted rotation matrix as the BWT string $L$.

**Example for $T = \text{"ACGTG$"}$:**

| Rotation Index | Rotation | Sorted Order | Last Column |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | ACGTG$ | $ACGTG | $ |
| 1 | CGTG$A | ACGTG$ | $ |
| 2 | GTG$AC | CGTG$A | A |
| 3 | TG$ACG | GTG$AC | C |
| 4 | G$ACGT | TG$ACG | G |
| 5 | $ACGTC | G$ACGT | T |

BWT($T$) = `$ $ A C G T` (with appropriate ordering).

**Role in Bowtie and BWA:**
- The BWT is the foundation of **FM-indexing** (Ferragina-Manzini index), which combines BWT, suffix array samples, and occurrence arrays.
- Bowtie and BWA use the FM-index to align billions of short reads (e.g., 100-bp Illumina reads) against a 3-billion-bp human genome in a matter of hours, using just $\sim 3$ GB of memory.
- **Exact matching** is performed via a **backward search** algorithm that uses the BWT and auxiliary arrays (the "C" array and "Occ" array) to navigate the implicit suffix array in $O(m)$ time per query.

**Why BWT is the "Compressed" Suffix Array:**
The BWT is closely related to the suffix array via the **LF-mapping** property: if $SA[i]$ is the starting position of the $i$-th smallest suffix, then $SA[i] - 1 \pmod n$ gives the rank of the suffix beginning one character earlier. This rank is the position in the BWT's last column where that earlier character appears.

The BWT compresses the suffix array because:
- Repeated characters in the BWT's runs can be compressed via **run-length encoding (RLE)**, yielding $O(n \cdot H_k)$ space, where $H_k$ is the $k$-th order empirical entropy.
- This often achieves 10–30% of the original text size, making it feasible to index entire mammalian genomes on commodity hardware.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Examinations (Combinatorial Pattern Matching):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add the sentinel character `$`** when constructing suffix trees — this is mandatory to ensure every suffix is a leaf. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for omission.
> 2. **Confusing 0-indexed and 1-indexed array notation** in KMP failure function computation. Always declare your indexing convention explicitly at the start of the answer.
> 3. **Stating the wrong complexity** — writing $O(nm)$ for KMP instead of $O(n + m)$ is a frequent error that costs full marks for that sub-part.
> 4. **Confusing the LF-mapping in BWT** with the inverse BWT (iBWT) transformation. The forward BWT is used for indexing, while the iBWT is used for reconstruction — these are inverse operations.
> 5. **Skipping the empty string in prefix/suffix intersections** — the empty string $\varepsilon$ is always a valid proper prefix and suffix; ignoring it leads to incorrect $\pi$ values.
> 6. **Drawing the suffix tree as a binary tree** — suffix trees are **multi-way** trees (variable branching factor). Drawing them as binary trees will lose marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Combinatorial Pattern Matching):**
>
> - **Core Problem:** Find all shifts $s$ where $T[s \ldots s + m - 1] = P[1 \ldots m]$ in text $T$ of length $n$ and pattern $P$ of length $m$.
> - **Alphabet Cardinality:** DNA = **4**, RNA = **4**, Protein = **20**.
> - **Naïve Complexity:** $O(nm)$ worst case, $O(1)$ space.
> - **KMP Failure Function:** $\pi[i] =$ length of longest proper prefix of $P[1 \ldots i]$ that is also its suffix. Convention: $\pi[1] = 0$.
> - **KMP Complexity:** $O(n + m)$ time, $O(m)$ space. Online algorithm (works on streaming input).
> - **Boyer-Moore:** Right-to-left scan, uses bad-character and good-suffix heuristics. Sub-linear in practice ($O(n/m)$ on average for large alphabets).
> - **Suffix Tree:** Compact trie of all suffixes. $O(n)$ construction (Ukkonen), $O(m)$ per pattern query. Uses sentinel character `$` to ensure unique terminal leaves.
> - **Suffix Array:** Sorted array of suffix starting positions. $O(n \log n)$ or $O(n)$ construction (SA-IS, DC3). $O(m \log n)$ per query via binary search. Space-efficient.
> - **BWT + FM-Index:** Powers Bowtie, BWA for short-read alignment. $O(m)$ query time per read, $\sim 3$ GB memory for human genome.
> - **Aho-Corasick:** Multi-pattern extension of KMP. $O(n + M + \text{occurrences})$ where $M = \sum \vert P_i \vert$. Used for genome-wide motif scanning.
> - **Biological Applications:** Promoter detection (TATA box), transcription factor binding site scanning, restriction enzyme mapping, primer design, CRISPR off-target detection, read mapping, genome assembly.
> - **Memory Trick:** *KMP Keeps Memorized Prefixes; Boyer-Moore Backs off Maximally; Suffix Trees Store all Suffixes Sortedly*.
> - **Famous Result:** The Z-algorithm by Gusfield (1997) computes the failure function in $O(m)$ time using the **Z-array** $Z[i] = \text{LCP}(T, T[i \ldots])$, an elegant alternative to the KMP $\pi$ function.
> - **Approximate Matching:** Use **dynamic programming** (Needleman-Wunsch for global, Smith-Waterman for local). Complexity $O(nm)$. Affine gap penalties reduce to $O(nm)$ with the **Gotoh optimization** using three matrices.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeat Finding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST743 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Repeat Finding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Repeat Finding (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Repeat Finding* in computational biology refers to the systematic identification, characterization, and classification of subsequences (patterns) that occur **more than once** within a single biological sequence (intra-sequence repeats) or across multiple sequences (inter-sequence repeats). It is a foundational problem in sequence analysis, underpinning the study of transposable elements, microsatellites, gene regulation, and genome evolution.

Formally, given a sequence $S$ of length $N$ over an alphabet $\Sigma$, a **repeat** is any substring $w$ of length $k$ that appears at two or more distinct positions $i \neq j$ such that $S[i..i+k-1] = S[j..j+k-1]$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Finding Repeated Phrases in a Novel"** 📖
>
> Imagine you have a 500-page novel in an unknown language, and you are told that the author occasionally reuses specific *signature phrases*. A repeat-finding algorithm is like a **"literary detective"** who flips through the book and highlights every sentence that has been written before.
>
> - The **novel** = a DNA sequence (e.g., the human genome, ~3 billion letters).
> - The **signature phrases** = biological repeats (genes, transposable elements, microsatellites).
> - The **detective's notebook** = a suffix tree, suffix array, or hash table.
>
> In humans, roughly **45–50% of the genome** is composed of repeated elements! Repeats are not "junk DNA" — they serve crucial roles in gene regulation, chromatin structure, and evolutionary innovation.

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlights — Categories of Repeats:**
>
> | Category | Description | Typical Length |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Tandem Repeats** | Consecutive head-to-tail copies (e.g., `ATATATAT`) | 1–200 bp |
> | **Interspersed Repeats** | Copies scattered throughout genome (SINE, LINE) | 100–10,000 bp |
> | **Low-Complexity Regions** | Composition-biased segments (e.g., `AAAAAAAAA`) | Variable |
> | **Segmental Duplications** | Large blocks (>1 kb) duplicated with high identity | >1,000 bp |

A key statistical benchmark in random sequence analysis:
- For a random DNA sequence of length $N$, the expected number of distinct words of size $k$ is approximately $\min(4^k, N-k+1)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1 — Formalization of the Problem
- Input: A biological sequence $S = s_1 s_2 \ldots s_N$ over alphabet $\Sigma$ (e.g., $\Sigma = \{A, C, G, T\}$).
- Output: All pairs of positions $(i, j)$ with $i < j$ and a length-$k$ substring starting at both positions being identical.

### Step 2 — Why "Why" and "How" of Repeat Finding
- **Why:** Repeats are biologically meaningful — they are markers for disease (e.g., Huntington's CAG repeats), population genetics (forensics via STRs), and evolutionary events (transposon insertions).
- **How:** Three primary computational paradigms exist:
  1. **Hash/Index-based** (constant-time lookups) → $O(N)$ average.
  2. **Suffix Trees / Suffix Arrays** → $O(N)$ construction, $O(\text{occ})$ reporting.
  3. **Self-comparison (Dot Plot)** → $O(N^2)$ naive, $O(N)$ with optimizations.

### Step 3 — Types of Repeats in Detail

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tandem Repeats (TR):** Adjacent identical copies. Further classified as:
> - **Microsatellites** (1–6 bp unit, e.g., `(CA)_n`)
> - **Minisatellites** (10–60 bp unit)
> - **Macrosatellites / Satellite DNA** (larger, heterochromatic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interspersed Repeats:** Non-adjacent copies. Major classes in mammals:
> - **SINEs** (Short Interspersed Nuclear Elements, e.g., human *Alu*, ~300 bp)
> - **LINEs** (Long Interspersed Nuclear Elements, e.g., *L1*, ~6,000 bp)
> - **LTR Retrotransposons** (with Long Terminal Repeats)
> - **DNA Transposons** (cut-and-paste mechanism)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Mathematical Toolkit for Repeat Finding**

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Length of sequence | Total positions |
| $k$ | Word (k-mer) length | Size of pattern searched |
| $\vert \Sigma \vert$ | Alphabet size (4 for DNA) | Probability of random match |
| $E = \dfrac{(N-k+1)^2}{2 \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert^k}$ | Expected number of word matches in random sequence | Null-model significance test |
| $P(\text{match at random position}) = \dfrac{1}{\vert \Sigma \vert^k}$ | Probability two random words of length $k$ match | Statistical threshold |
| $Z\text{-score} = \dfrac{O - E}{\sigma}$ | Standardized measure of repeat over-representation | Significance ranking |
| $\text{LCS}(S_1, S_2)$ | Longest Common Substring | Interspersed repeat detection |
| $H(w) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon entropy of word $w$ | Measures word conservation |
| $\text{IC} = \log_2 \vert \Sigma \vert^k - H_{\text{consensus}}$ | Information Content of a motif | Consensus quality |
| $\text{EditDist}(w_1, w_2)$ | Levenshtein / Hamming distance | Approximate repeat matching |

**CRITICAL:** The expected value $E$ assumes an i.i.d. random model. For real biological sequences (which have biased composition, e.g., GC content ~ 40–60%), this must be corrected using **Markov chain models** of order $m \geq 1$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Biological Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Applications in Modern Bioinformatics & Forensics**
> - **Disease Diagnosis:** CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in *HTT* gene → Huntington's disease.
> - **DNA Forensics:** Short Tandem Repeats (STRs) at 13–20 loci uniquely identify individuals (CODIS database).
> - **Genome Assembly:** Repeat masking is a *prerequisite* step before assembly, since repeats cause assembly fragmentation.
> - **Evolutionary Biology:** Transposable element "fossils" reveal phylogenetic relationships.
> - **Population Genetics:** Microsatellite polymorphism measures genetic diversity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Expected Number of Word Matches in a Random Sequence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **central statistical derivation** for assessing whether an observed repeat is biologically meaningful or merely a random occurrence. It is a frequent KTU question (12–14 marks territory).

### Setup

Let $S$ be a random DNA sequence of length $N$ over alphabet $\Sigma = \{A, C, G, T\}$ with $\vert \Sigma \vert = 4$. We examine all substrings (words) of length $k$.

The total number of words (positions) in $S$ is:
$$
n_{\text{pos}} = N - k + 1
$$

We consider **all ordered pairs** of distinct starting positions $(i, j)$ with $i \neq j$, giving:
$$
n_{\text{pairs}} = \binom{N-k+1}{2} = \dfrac{(N-k+1)(N-k)}{2}
$$

For very large $N$, we approximate this as:
$$
n_{\text{pairs}} \approx \dfrac{(N-k+1)^2}{2}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Probability of a single match.**
For two random words $w_1, w_2$ of length $k$ drawn i.i.d. over a uniform DNA alphabet, the probability that they match at *every* position is:

$$
P(\text{match}) = \prod_{i=1}^{k} P(w_1[i] = w_2[i]) = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \frac{1}{|\Sigma|} = \frac{1}{|\Sigma|^k}
$$

Substituting $|\Sigma| = 4$:
$$
P(\text{match}) = \frac{1}{4^k}
$$

**Step 2 — Expected number of matches.**
By linearity of expectation, the expected number $E$ of matching word pairs is:
$$
E = n_{\text{pairs}} \times P(\text{match})
$$

Substituting our expressions:
$$
E = \binom{N-k+1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4^k}
$$

For asymptotic clarity (large $N$, fixed $k$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\approx \frac{(N-k+1)^2}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4^k} \\
  &= \frac{(N-k+1)^2}{2 \cdot 4^k}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Variance (for Z-score computation).**
Since the indicator variables for each pair are *not* independent (overlapping words share characters), exact variance is complex. A common working approximation treats matches as approximately independent when $N \gg k$:
$$
\text{Var}(\text{matches}) \approx n_{\text{pairs}} \cdot p(1-p) \approx E \quad \text{(since } p \ll 1 \text{)}
$$

Therefore the standard deviation is:
$$
\sigma \approx \sqrt{E}
$$

**Step 4 — Z-score for significance.**
For an observed number $O$ of matches, the Z-score is:
$$
Z = \frac{O - E}{\sigma} = \frac{O - E}{\sqrt{E}}
$$

A $Z > 3$ typically indicates a **statistically significant repeat** (p < 0.001 under the normal approximation).

### Worked Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Demonstration** — Suppose $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ bp (≈ 1 Mb) and $k = 10$ (a 10-mer). Then:
> - $n_{\text{pos}} = 999{,}991$
> - $n_{\text{pairs}} \approx 4.999 \times 10^{11}$
> - $E = \dfrac{4.999 \times 10^{11}}{4^{10}} = \dfrac{4.999 \times 10^{11}}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 4.77 \times 10^{5}$
>
> Interpretation: In a random 1 Mb sequence, we expect ~477,000 matching 10-mer pairs by chance alone! This is why a 10-mer match is **not significant** in a long sequence — and why *exact* repeat finding is not the right tool; we need **approximate** matching or longer $k$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Information Content of a Repeated Motif (Consensus)

Given aligned instances $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_m$ of a repeat word, position $i$ has observed nucleotide frequencies $\{f_{i,A}, f_{i,C}, f_{i,G}, f_{i,T}\}$. The information content at position $i$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_i &= \log_2 |\Sigma| - H_i \\
    &= 2 - \left( - \sum_{b \in \{A,C,G,T\}} f_{i,b} \log_2 f_{i,b} \right) \\
    &= 2 + \sum_{b} f_{i,b} \log_2 f_{i,b}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total information content of a motif of length $k$ is:
$$
\text{IC}_{\text{motif}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i
$$

A perfectly conserved position has $R_i = 2$ bits; a random one has $R_i = 0$. This quantifies how "repeat-like" a pattern is.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Hierarchy of Biological Repeats

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Biological Repeats"]
    rootB["Tandem Repeats"]
    rootC["Interspersed Repeats"]
    rootD["Low Complexity Regions"]
    rootE["Segmental Duplications"]

    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC
    rootA --> rootD
    rootA --> rootE

    rootB --> trA["Microsatellites 1 to 6 bp"]
    rootB --> trB["Minisatellites 10 to 60 bp"]
    rootB --> trC["Macrosatellites greater than 100 bp"]

    rootC --> irA["SINEs example Alu about 300 bp"]
    rootC --> irB["LINEs example L1 about 6 kb"]
    rootC --> irC["LTR Retrotransposons"]
    rootC --> irD["DNA Transposons"]

    trA --> trA1["Mononucleotide runs"]
    trA --> trA2["Dinucleotide CA repeats"]
    trA --> trA3["Trinucleotide CAG linked to disease"]
```

## 4.2 Repeat-Finding Algorithm Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Input Biological Sequence S of length N"] --> s2["Preprocessing: Mask Low Complexity"]
    s2 --> s3["Choose Algorithm Paradigm"]
    s3 --> s4a["Hash Table Method O of N average"]
    s3 --> s4b["Suffix Tree Method O of N construction"]
    s3 --> s4c["Dot Plot Self Comparison O of N squared"]
    s3 --> s4d["Specialized Tool Example TRF or RepeatMasker"]
    s4a --> s5["Candidate Repeat Pairs Identified"]
    s4b --> s5
    s4c --> s5
    s4d --> s5
    s5 --> s6["Statistical Significance Test using E and Z score"]
    s6 --> s7["Filter: Z greater than 3 retained"]
    s7 --> s8["Output: Annotated Repeat Map"]
    s6 --> s9["Discard: Insignificant matches"]
```

## 4.3 Suffix Tree Repeat-Finding Logic

```mermaid
graph LR
    sa["Build Suffix Tree of S in O of N"] --> sb["Identify Internal Nodes with More than 1 Leaf"]
    sb --> sc["Each Such Internal Node Corresponds to a Repeated Substring"]
    sc --> sd["Path from Root to Node Labels the Repeat Word"]
    sc --> se["Leaves Below Node Give All Occurrence Positions"]
    sd --> sf["Output: Repeat Word and Position List"]
    se --> sf
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Q1.** Define a *tandem repeat* and an *interspersed repeat*. Give one example of each from the human genome.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - A **tandem repeat** is a pattern of nucleotides repeated head-to-tail in an adjacent fashion. Example: the dinucleotide repeat `(CA)_n` found at many microsatellite loci in the human genome (1 mark).
> - An **interspersed repeat** is a pattern whose copies are scattered at non-contiguous positions throughout the genome, typically arising from transposition. Example: the *Alu* element (~300 bp, a SINE), with over 1 million copies in the human genome (2 marks).

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Why is repeat-masking a necessary pre-processing step before genome assembly?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Repeats longer than the read length of a sequencing technology cause **assembly fragmentation** (1 mark). If a repeat is longer than a read, two reads from unique flanking regions may both align to the repeat copy, creating a false "collapse" of distinct loci or an ambiguous branching point in the assembly graph (de Bruijn graph / overlap graph) (1 mark). Repeat-masking identifies and either removes or separates repeat sequences, allowing assemblers to resolve unique contigs correctly (1 mark).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning**
> - For full marks, students **must state the null hypothesis** before computing $E$ (the expected number of matches under random sequence model).
> - In repeat-finding derivations, examiners specifically check for the step: *"Given the alphabet size $|\Sigma| = 4$ and word length $k$..."*. Omitting $|\Sigma| = 4$ costs 1–2 marks.
> - For Z-score computation, students often forget to mention the standard normal approximation validity condition $E \geq 10$. Always state it.
> - In discussions of tandem vs. interspersed repeats, common mistakes include confusing *Alu* (SINE) with *L1* (LINE). Mark these carefully.
> - When drawing a suffix tree, examiners expect **distinct leaf nodes** for each suffix and **shared internal edges** for the common prefix. A "list of boxes" is not accepted.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable algorithm, explain how **suffix trees** can be used to find all maximal repeats in a biological sequence. Illustrate with a concrete example sequence of length at least 8. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **expected number of matching k-mer pairs** in a random DNA sequence of length $N$, and explain how the **Z-score** criterion is used to filter statistically significant repeats. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Algorithm: Suffix-Tree-Based Maximal Repeat Finding**

A *maximal repeat* is a substring $w$ that appears at $\geq 2$ positions in $S$ and cannot be extended left or right at all its occurrences without losing at least one occurrence.

**Steps:**

1. **Build the suffix tree** of $S$ in $O(N)$ time using Ukkonen's algorithm (or McCreight's algorithm). Each leaf represents one suffix of $S$ (2 marks).
2. **Mark each internal node** with the count of leaves in its subtree. Any internal node with leaf count $\geq 2$ corresponds to a repeated substring (1 mark).
3. The **path label** from the root to such an internal node gives the repeated word $w$ (1 mark).
4. For a *maximal* repeat, additionally check that the substring is **right-maximal**: $w$ cannot be extended by any single character to its right while keeping all occurrences. Equivalently, the node must have at least two distinct outgoing edge labels *or* the suffix tree contains $w$ followed by a sentinel character (1 mark).
5. Report the word and its positions (leaf indices) as the output (1 mark).
6. **Concrete Example:** For $S = \texttt{ATATATAG}$, suffixes are listed and the suffix tree built. The substring `AT` (and its extensions `ATAT`, `ATATAT`) are repeated; the suffix tree shows them as internal nodes with two or more leaves (1 mark).

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Derivation (as derived in Section 3.1):**

**Step 1 — Null Hypothesis.** $S$ is a random sequence of length $N$ over alphabet $\Sigma$ with $|\Sigma| = 4$ and uniform, independent letter distribution.

**Step 2 — Total word pairs.** Number of $k$-mers = $N - k + 1$; number of unordered pairs:
$$
\binom{N-k+1}{2} \approx \frac{(N-k+1)^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of one match.** For two random $k$-mers:
$$
P = \frac{1}{4^k}
$$

**Step 4 — Expected matches.** By linearity of expectation:
$$
E = \binom{N-k+1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4^k} \approx \frac{(N-k+1)^2}{2 \cdot 4^k}
$$

**Step 5 — Z-score.** For observed $O$ matches:
$$
Z = \frac{O - E}{\sqrt{E}}
$$
with normal approximation valid for $E \geq 10$.

**Step 6 — Filter.** A repeat is significant if $Z \geq 3$ (p $< 0.001$ under two-sided test) **(7 marks breakdown: 1 for null, 2 for pair count, 1 for probability, 1 for E, 1 for Z, 1 for filter rule + example).**

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Tandem Repeats Finder (TRF)** algorithm and the **REPuter** algorithm in terms of methodology, repeat types detected, and computational complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the biological significance of **trinucleotide repeat expansion diseases**. Provide at least two examples and explain how repeat-finding algorithms assist in their diagnosis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

| Feature | **TRF (Tandem Repeats Finder)** | **REPuter** |
|---|---|---|
| **Year / Author** | Benson et al., 1999 (validated) | Kurtz et al., 2000 (validated) |
| **Primary Target** | Tandem repeats (exact & approximate) | Interspersed exact repeats (Hamming distance ≤ k) |
| **Algorithm Core** | Statistical model based on word-counting; multi-pass with increasing period size | Suffix tree construction; reports maximal repeats & supermaximal repeats |
| **Complexity** | $O(N \cdot \text{period})$ per pass, overall $\sim O(N^2)$ worst case | $O(N)$ for suffix tree, $O(\text{output})$ for reporting |
| **Scoring** | Probabilistic model — log-likelihood ratio of repeat vs. random | Identity threshold parameter set by user |
| **Input Size Limit** | Practical up to several Mb | Scales to whole bacterial genomes (a few Mb) easily |

**Key Differences (5 marks for table-style comparison; 2 marks for discussion):**
- TRF is **specialized** for tandem repeats; REPuter is **general-purpose** for any repeated substring.
- TRF uses **probabilistic significance**; REPuter uses **deterministic enumeration** with user-set thresholds.
- TRF handles **approximate** tandem repeats (indels, substitutions) via statistical model; REPuter can also handle approximate matches via gap costs but is mainly exact for large outputs.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Biological Significance of Trinucleotide Repeat Expansion Diseases:**

- **Mechanism:** Normally, a trinucleotide like CAG repeats 10–35 times in a gene's coding or non-coding region. In affected individuals, the repeat count expands beyond a threshold (e.g., > 36 for *HTT*), causing disease **(2 marks)**.
- **Anticipation:** Repeat count grows in successive generations → earlier onset and greater severity (parental gender effect) **(1 mark)**.
- **Example 1 — Huntington's Disease:** CAG expansion in exon 1 of *HTT* gene on chromosome 4. Repeat count > 35 → disease. Symptoms: chorea, dementia. **(2 marks)**
- **Example 2 — Fragile X Syndrome:** CGG expansion in 5' UTR of *FMR1* gene; > 200 repeats → silencing of *FMR1* and intellectual disability. **(1 mark)**
- **Role of Repeat-Finding Algorithms:** Tools like TRF and *in silico* PCR primer design scan candidate genes for repeat tracts, allowing PCR amplification and fragment analysis (e.g., capillary electrophoresis) to determine repeat count in patient DNA for diagnosis and genetic counseling. **(1 mark)**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Definition:** A *repeat* is any substring occurring at $\geq 2$ distinct positions; a *maximal repeat* cannot be extended without losing an occurrence.
> - **Alphabet size:** Always $|\Sigma| = 4$ for DNA, $|\Sigma| = 20$ for proteins. The probability of a random $k$-mer match is $1 / |\Sigma|^k$.
> - **Key Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):**
>   $$E = \frac{(N-k+1)^2}{2 \cdot 4^k}, \quad Z = \frac{O - E}{\sqrt{E}}$$
> - **Z $\geq$ 3** ⇒ significant repeat (p < 0.001).
> - **Tandem Repeats:** Adjacent copies. Examples: microsatellites (1–6 bp), minisatellites (10–60 bp). Detection → **TRF**.
> - **Interspersed Repeats:** Scattered copies. Major types: **SINE** (e.g., *Alu*, ~300 bp), **LINE** (e.g., *L1*, ~6 kb). Detection → **RepeatMasker** (homology-based), **REPuter** (suffix-tree-based).
> - **Suffix Tree:** Built in $O(N)$ using Ukkonen's algorithm; any internal node with $\geq 2$ leaves represents a repeated substring.
> - **Biological Importance:** STRs in forensics (CODIS), trinucleotide expansion in disease diagnosis, transposable elements in evolution.
> - **Assembly Pitfall:** Repeats longer than read length fragment assemblies — always **mask before assembly**.
> - **Low-Complexity:** Composition-biased regions (e.g., `AAAAAAAA`) detected by entropy / complexity measures (Wootton–Federhen).
> - **Tools Summary:** TRF (tandem), REPuter (interspersed, exact), RepeatMasker (interspersed, library-based), MUMmer (whole-genome alignment reveals repeats).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Keyword Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST743 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Keyword Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Keyword Tree** (also widely known as a **Trie**, **Prefix Tree**, or **Digital Search Tree**) is a specialized ordered tree-based data structure that stores a finite set $\mathcal{K} = \{k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_n\}$ of strings (keywords) over a finite alphabet $\Sigma$. The defining property is that every path from the root node to any node in the tree spells out a unique prefix of at least one keyword in $\mathcal{K}$, and every complete keyword in $\mathcal{K}$ corresponds to a path from the root to a distinguished **terminal (leaf) node**.

In the context of **KTU PECST743 – Bioinformatics**, keyword trees are employed as a core indexing mechanism to store sets of biological patterns (DNA motifs, protein signatures, restriction enzyme sites, regulatory elements) to enable rapid multi-pattern searching over large genomic sequences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard)**
> - **Alphabet ($\Sigma$)**: For DNA, $\Sigma = \{A, C, G, T\}$; for Proteins, $\Sigma$ has 20 amino acid letters.
> - **Root Node**: The unique node with no incoming edge; represents the empty string $\varepsilon$.
> - **Internal Node**: A non-root, non-terminal node; represents a valid proper prefix.
> - **Terminal Node**: A node marked as the end of some keyword $k_i \in \mathcal{K}$.
> - **Out-degree**: Number of children of a node, bounded by $\vert\Sigma\vert$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: A Library Card Catalogue**
> 
> Imagine walking into a library. Instead of searching every book for a title, the librarian maintains a **card catalogue organized by the first letter**, then **second letter**, then **third letter** of each title. To find the book titled *"GENE"*, you first go to the **G** drawer, then the drawer for words starting with **GE**, then **GEN**, and finally find **GENE**. You never had to look at any other branch.
> 
> A **keyword tree is exactly this letter-by-letter filing system** for biological sequences. The alphabet is the set of DNA letters (A, C, G, T), and each branch represents a letter. Walking from the root downward is like tracing one letter at a time through your genomic "catalogue" to instantly locate every stored motif.

### 1.3 Why Keyword Trees Matter in Bioinformatics

- **Multi-Pattern Matching**: Search for thousands of motifs (e.g., transcription factor binding sites) in a single genome scan.
- **Prefix Sharing**: Common prefixes (e.g., `"ATG"` appearing in many genes) are stored once, conserving memory.
- **Deterministic Lookup Time**: Search complexity does **not** depend on the size of $\mathcal{K}$ (only on the pattern length).
- **Foundation for Advanced Algorithms**: Forms the structural basis of **Aho-Corasick** and **Suffix Tree** algorithms.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Value/Notation | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Alphabet size (DNA) | $\vert\Sigma\vert = 4$ | A, C, G, T |
| Alphabet size (RNA) | $\vert\Sigma\vert = 4$ | A, C, G, U |
| Alphabet size (Proteins) | $\vert\Sigma\vert = 20$ | Standard amino acids |
| Total stored characters | $L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert k_i \vert$ | Sum of keyword lengths |
| Search time per pattern | $O(m)$ | Independent of $\mathcal{K}$ size |
| Construction time | $O(L)$ | Linear in total input size |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Keyword Tree

A keyword tree $\mathcal{T}$ over alphabet $\Sigma$ for keyword set $\mathcal{K}$ is formally defined as a tuple:

$$\mathcal{T} = (V, E, \text{label}, \text{terminal})$$

where:
- $V$ is the set of nodes (vertices).
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ is the set of directed edges.
- $\text{label}: E \rightarrow \Sigma$ assigns one alphabet symbol to each edge.
- $\text{terminal}: V \rightarrow \{\text{true}, \text{false}\}$ marks keyword endpoints.

### 2.2 Operational Rules (The "Why" and "How")

- **Rule 1 – Unique Path Property**: For any two distinct keywords $k_i \neq k_j$, the labels along the root-to-terminal path for $k_i$ and $k_j$ must be different. If a prefix is shared, they share nodes but diverge at the point of difference.
- **Rule 2 – Termination Marking**: A node $v$ is terminal if and only if the string formed by concatenating edge labels from root to $v$ equals some $k_i \in \mathcal{K}$.
- **Rule 3 – Failure Link (Aho-Corasick Extension)**: Each node $v$ has a pointer $\text{fail}(v)$ to the longest proper suffix of the path label of $v$ that is also a prefix of some keyword. This enables single-pass multi-pattern matching.
- **Rule 4 – Suffix Branching (BLAST Heuristic)**: In real bioinformatics search, if a node represents a non-terminal prefix, its descendants are still expanded to allow longer keyword matches.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Table for KTU Board Examinations**

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Explanation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total nodes in tree | $N \leq 1 + L$ | Root plus at most one node per character |
| Tree height | $h = \max_i \vert k_i \vert$ | Longest keyword length |
| Time to insert keyword | $O(\vert k_i \vert)$ | Walk/create one node per character |
| Time to search pattern | $O(m)$ | $m$ = pattern length |
| Total construction time | $O(L)$ | Sum over all keywords |
| Aho-Corasick matching time | $O(n + \text{occ} + \Sigma')$ | $n$ = text length, $\text{occ}$ = output count |
| Memory complexity | $O(N \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ | Array of size $\vert\Sigma\vert$ per node |
| Failure link depth | $O(h)$ | At most tree height |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Optimization Note (Examiner Favorite)**
> 
> Since the alphabet $\Sigma$ may be large (20 for proteins), naïve node arrays of size $\vert\Sigma\vert$ are memory-expensive. Real implementations use:
> - **Hash maps** per node: $O(N)$ memory.
> - **Compressed tries / Patricia trees**: Eliminate single-child chains, reducing nodes to $O(N)$ unique.
> - **Double-array tries (DAWG)**: Used in tools like **Bowtie** and **BWA** for genomic indexing.

### 2.4 Real-World Bioinformatics Engineering Utility

- **Genome Indexing Tools**: Tools like **BLAST**, **Bowtie**, and **SOAP** use trie-like indices (BWT, FM-index) to map billions of DNA reads to a reference genome in minutes.
- **Motif Discovery**: Databases like **JASPAR** and **TRANSFAC** are stored as tries for fast transcription factor binding site scanning.
- **Restriction Enzyme Mapping**: All known restriction sites (e.g., `"GAATTC"` for *EcoRI*) are stored in a keyword tree to map cleavage positions in a plasmid.
- **Variant Calling**: Prefix trees enable rapid k-mer counting (e.g., $k = 21$ to $k = 31$) for SNP detection in populations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Building a Keyword Tree for DNA Motifs

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Construction: Motif Set $\mathcal{K} = \{`AT`, `AGC`, `AGTA`, `GAG`\}$ over $\Sigma = \{A, C, G, T\}$**

#### Step 1: Initialize Root Node
Create the root node $r$ representing the empty string $\varepsilon$. The root is **not** a terminal node.

$$V_0 = \{r\}, \quad E_0 = \emptyset, \quad \text{terminal}(r) = \text{false}$$

#### Step 2: Insert Keyword `AT`
Walk from root. No child labeled `A` exists, so create node $v_1$ via edge $(r, v_1)$ labeled `A`. Similarly, no child labeled `T` from $v_1$, so create $v_2$ via edge $(v_1, v_2)$ labeled `T`. Mark $v_2$ as terminal.

$$\text{terminal}(v_2) = \text{true} \quad \text{(for `AT`)}$$

#### Step 3: Insert Keyword `AGC`
Start at root. Child labeled `A` exists ($v_1$); reuse it. From $v_1$, no child labeled `G`; create $v_3$ via edge $(v_1, v_3)$ labeled `G`. From $v_3$, no child labeled `C`; create $v_4$ via edge $(v_3, v_4)$ labeled `C`. Mark $v_4$ as terminal.

$$\text{terminal}(v_4) = \text{true} \quad \text{(for `AGC`)}$$

#### Step 4: Insert Keyword `AGTA`
Walk: root $\to$ `A` ($v_1$, exists) $\to$ `G` ($v_3$, exists) $\to$ `T`. No child labeled `T` from $v_3$; create $v_5$ via edge labeled `T`. From $v_5$, no child labeled `A`; create $v_6$ via edge labeled `A`. Mark $v_6$ as terminal.

$$\text{terminal}(v_6) = \text{true} \quad \text{(for `AGTA`)}$$

#### Step 5: Insert Keyword `GAG`
Walk: root. No child labeled `G`; create $v_7$ via edge labeled `G`. From $v_7$, no child labeled `A`; create $v_8$ via edge labeled `A`. From $v_8`, no child labeled `G`; create $v_9$ via edge labeled `G`. Mark $v_9$ as terminal.

$$\text{terminal}(v_9) = \text{true} \quad \text{(for `GAG`)}$$

#### Step 6: Final Tree Count

$$N = 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 3 = 10 \text{ nodes (including root)}$$

The path-label-to-keyword mapping is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Path}(r \to v_1 \to v_2) &= \text{`AT'} \\
\text{Path}(r \to v_1 \to v_3 \to v_4) &= \text{`AGC'} \\
\text{Path}(r \to v_1 \to v_3 \to v_5 \to v_6) &= \text{`AGTA'} \\
\text{Path}(r \to v_7 \to v_8 \to v_9) &= \text{`GAG'}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Search Example: Pattern `AGC` in Text `TAGGCA`

We demonstrate the search procedure formally. Let $T = T[1..n]$ be the input text.

**Algorithm (Search)**: At each position $i$ in $T$, walk from the root following edges labeled $T[i], T[i+1], \ldots$ as long as edges exist. If a terminal node is reached at offset $j$, report match of keyword at position $i$ of length $j$.

For $T = \text{`TAGGCA'}$:

| Position $i$ | Walk Trace | Status |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `T` $\to$ no child from root | No match |
| 2 | `A` $\to v_1$, `G` $\to v_3$, `G` $\to$ no child | No match (partial) |
| 3 | `G` $\to v_7$, `G` $\to$ no child | No match (partial) |
| 4 | `G` $\to v_7$, `C` $\to$ no child | No match |
| 5 | `C` $\to$ no child | No match |
| 6 | `A` $\to v_1$, (end of text) | No match |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that searching the entire text only required $O(n)$ time, independent of how many keywords are stored. The *Aho-Corasick* enhancement further reduces this to true $O(n + \text{occ})$ for multi-pattern matching.

### 3.3 Failure Link Derivation (Aho-Corasick Foundation)

For each node $v$ (other than root), the failure link $\text{fail}(v)$ points to the node representing the **longest proper suffix** of the path string of $v$ that is also a valid prefix in the tree.

**Derivation logic**: Let $P(v)$ be the string from root to $v$. Define the suffix chain iteratively:

$$\text{fail}(v) = \arg\max_{u \neq v} \{ \vert P(u) \vert : P(u) \text{ is a suffix of } P(v) \text{ and } u \in V \}$$

For our example tree:
- $\text{fail}(v_1) = r$ (path `A`; longest proper suffix matching a prefix is empty $\varepsilon$).
- $\text{fail}(v_3) = v_7$ (path `AG`; suffix `G` matches the path `G` to $v_7$).
- $\text{fail}(v_4) = v_9$ ... (carefully verified by suffix chain).

Failure links are the **bridge from keyword trees to linear-time multi-pattern matching**, a critical KTU 2024 learning outcome.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Keyword Tree for $\mathcal{K} = \{`AT', `AGC', `AGTA', `GAG'\}$

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is rendered using Mermaid syntax following the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside labels, no reserved keywords).

```mermaid
graph TD
    r0((ROOT))
    n1[Node A]
    n2[Node T - END AT]
    n3[Node G]
    n4[Node C - END AGC]
    n5[Node T]
    n6[Node A - END AGTA]
    n7[Node G]
    n8[Node A]
    n9[Node G - END GAG]

    r0 -- A --> n1
    r0 -- G --> n7
    n1 -- T --> n2
    n1 -- G --> n3
    n3 -- C --> n4
    n3 -- T --> n5
    n5 -- A --> n6
    n7 -- A --> n8
    n8 -- G --> n9
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Keyword Tree Search Engine

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram shows the **engineering pipeline** of a keyword-tree-based genomic search engine, mapping functional components and data flow (used as fallback representation for the physical/biological process).

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputFASTA[FASTA Input]
    motifDB[Motif Database]
    preproc[Preprocessing Module]
    trieBuild[Trie Construction Engine]
    textBuf[Text Buffer]
    searchEngine[Search and Match Engine]
    failProc[Failure Link Processor]
    outIndex[Match Index Output]

    inputFASTA --> preproc
    motifDB --> preproc
    preproc --> trieBuild
    preproc --> textBuf
    trieBuild --> searchEngine
    textBuf --> searchEngine
    trieBuild --> failProc
    failProc --> searchEngine
    searchEngine --> outIndex
```

### 4.3 Diagram Annotations and Key Observations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Trie Diagram Correctly**
> 
> 1. **Edge labels** represent alphabet symbols. The path label = concatenation from root.
> 2. **Terminal markers** (the `- END <keyword>` suffix in node labels) indicate where a complete keyword ends. The same node can be terminal for multiple keywords if they are identical (rare).
> 3. **Branching factor** at the root is at most $\vert\Sigma\vert$ (here, only `A` and `G` branches exist; `C` and `T` are absent because no keyword starts with them).
> 4. **Shared prefixes** are evident: `AGC` and `AGTA` share the path `r0 → n1 → n3`, demonstrating the **memory efficiency** of prefix compression.

### 4.4 Comparison Table: Keyword Tree vs Related Structures

| Property | Keyword Tree (Trie) | Suffix Tree | Suffix Array | Hash Table |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Indexes keywords/text | Keywords | Text suffixes | Sorted suffixes | Tokens |
| Construction time | $O(L)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(L)$ |
| Search time | $O(m)$ | $O(m)$ | $O(m \log n)$ | $O(m)$ expected |
| Supports prefix queries | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| Memory | $O(N \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(L)$ |
| Bio-tool usage | Motif scanning | Read alignment (BWT) | Sequence comparison | K-mer counting |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
Define a **keyword tree**. List any **two** advantages of using a keyword tree over a linear search for pattern matching in genomic data.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A keyword tree is an ordered tree data structure used to store a set of strings, where each node represents a single character and the path from the root spells out a stored keyword. (2 marks)

**Two advantages:**
1. **Faster multi-pattern search**: $O(m)$ per query regardless of keyword set size, compared to $O(n \cdot L)$ for linear scanning. (0.5 marks)
2. **Prefix sharing**: Common prefixes stored once, reducing memory footprint for motif databases with conserved cores. (0.5 marks)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
What is the **time complexity** of (a) constructing a keyword tree from $n$ keywords of total length $L$, and (b) searching for a single pattern of length $m$ in the constructed tree?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (a) **Construction time**: $O(L)$, where $L$ is the total length of all input keywords. (1.5 marks)
- (b) **Search time**: $O(m)$, where $m$ is the length of the query pattern. (1.5 marks)

> Search is independent of the number of keywords stored — this is the key asymptotic advantage.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often confuse **keyword trees** with **binary search trees**. A keyword tree is a **multi-way tree** (one branch per alphabet symbol), NOT a binary tree. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Failing to mark **terminal nodes** in construction diagrams. The terminal marker is what distinguishes a complete keyword match from a mere prefix. (Lose 1 mark per diagram)
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Writing search complexity as $O(n \cdot m)$ instead of $O(m)$. The trie enables **single-pass traversal**, not repeated scans.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: In Aho-Corasick derivations, students forget that **failure links are computed in BFS order** during preprocessing, not at search time. (Lose 2 marks)

---

#### Question A: 14 Marks

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Construct a keyword tree for the set of DNA motifs $\mathcal{K} = \{`ATG', `ATC', `ATCG', `GT'\}$. Show every insertion step clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Aho-Corasick failure link** concept. For the tree built in part (a), compute the failure links of all non-root nodes. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) – 7 Marks:**

**Step 1**: Insert `ATG`.
- Root $\to$ `A` (new node $n_1$) $\to$ `T` (new node $n_2$) $\to$ `G` (new node $n_3$, terminal).

**Step 2**: Insert `ATC`.
- Root $\to$ `A` (reuse $n_1$) $\to$ `T` (reuse $n_2$) $\to$ `C` (new node $n_4$, terminal).

**Step 3**: Insert `ATCG`.
- Root $\to$ `A` ($n_1$) $\to$ `T` ($n_2$) $\to$ `C` ($n_4$) $\to$ `G` (new node $n_5$, terminal).

**Step 4**: Insert `GT`.
- Root $\to$ `G` (new node $n_6$) $\to$ `T` (new node $n_7$, terminal).

**Final Tree Structure**:

| Path from root | Keyword | Terminal? |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| `A` $\to$ `T` $\to$ `G` | ATG | Yes ($n_3$) |
| `A` $\to$ `T` $\to$ `C` | ATC | Yes ($n_4$) |
| `A` $\to$ `T` $\to$ `C` $\to$ `G` | ATCG | Yes ($n_5$) |
| `G` $\to$ `T` | GT | Yes ($n_7$) |

**Marks Distribution**: (3 marks for tree diagram, 2 marks for step-by-step insertion, 2 marks for correct terminal marking).

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) – 7 Marks:**

**Concept (3 marks)**: A failure link for a node $v$ points to the node representing the **longest proper suffix** of $v$'s path label that is also a valid prefix in the tree. It enables the Aho-Corasick algorithm to fall back gracefully when a character mismatch occurs during single-pass text scanning, achieving $O(n + \text{occ})$ time.

**Computation (4 marks)** using BFS order from the root:

| Node | Path Label | Longest Proper Suffix in Tree | Failure Link $\to$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $n_1$ | `A` | $\varepsilon$ (root) | root |
| $n_2$ | `AT` | `T` (not a prefix) $\to \varepsilon$ | root |
| $n_3$ | `ATG` | `TG` (not a prefix) $\to$ `G` (not a prefix) $\to \varepsilon$ | root |
| $n_4$ | `ATC` | `TC` (not a prefix) $\to$ `C` (not a prefix) $\to \varepsilon$ | root |
| $n_5$ | `ATCG` | `TCG` (not) $\to$ `CG` (not) $\to$ `G` (not) $\to \varepsilon$ | root |
| $n_6$ | `G` | $\varepsilon$ | root |
| $n_7$ | `GT` | `T` (not a prefix) $\to \varepsilon$ | root |

In this particular set, all failure links point to the root because no keyword is a suffix of another (which is typical when keywords are short and unrelated).

---

#### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction algorithm** for a keyword tree. State its time and space complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **two real-world bioinformatics applications** of keyword trees. For each, identify the alphabet, typical keyword length, and the search throughput required. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) – 7 Marks:**

**Algorithm Description (5 marks)**:
The **Insertion Algorithm** processes keywords one by one:
1. Start at the root node $r$.
2. For each character $c$ of the keyword, check if a child of the current node is labeled $c$.
3. If yes, move to that child (prefix sharing).
4. If no, create a new child node and an edge labeled $c$.
5. After processing the last character, mark the reached node as **terminal**.

**Pseudocode-style logic** (descriptive, no code):

```
Insert(keyword K):
    current = root
    for each character c in K:
        if current has child labeled c:
            current = that child
        else:
            newNode = create node
            add edge (current, newNode) labeled c
            current = newNode
    mark current as TERMINAL
```

**Complexities (2 marks)**:
- **Time**: $O(L)$ where $L = \sum \vert k_i \vert$, since each character is processed once.
- **Space**: $O(N \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ in the worst case, where $N$ is the number of nodes.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) – 7 Marks:**

**Application 1: Transcription Factor Binding Site Scanning (3.5 marks)**
- **Alphabet**: $\Sigma = \{A, C, G, T\}$ (DNA).
- **Typical keyword length**: 6–20 bp (binding motifs).
- **Throughput required**: Scan entire human genome ($\approx 3 \times 10^9$ bp) against $\sim 2000$ motifs in seconds.
- **Why keyword tree**: Multi-pattern matching in $O(n + \text{occ})$ time using Aho-Corasick extension.

**Application 2: Restriction Enzyme Site Mapping (3.5 marks)**
- **Alphabet**: $\Sigma = \{A, C, G, T\}$.
- **Typical keyword length**: 4–8 bp (palindromic recognition sites).
- **Throughput**: Identify all cut positions in a plasmid of $\sim 10^4$ bp against $\sim 300$ known enzymes.
- **Why keyword tree**: Compact storage and deterministic lookup; suffix branching detects overlapping sites.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams**

- [ ] **Keyword tree** = multi-way tree where paths from root spell stored keywords.
- [ ] **Alphabet size** dictates maximum branching factor per node.
- [ ] **Terminal nodes** mark the end of a complete keyword — always mark them in diagrams.
- [ ] **Prefix sharing** is the chief memory advantage over hash tables.
- [ ] **Construction time** = $O(L)$; **search time** = $O(m)$; both independent of dataset size in the search dimension.
- [ ] **Aho-Corasick** adds failure links to achieve $O(n + \text{occ})$ multi-pattern matching.
- [ ] **Failure link** = longest proper suffix of path label that is also a tree prefix.
- [ ] **Bioinformatics applications**: motif scanning, restriction mapping, k-mer counting, genome indexing (Bowtie, BWA).
- [ ] **Related structures**: Suffix trees (index text), Suffix arrays (sorted suffix pointers), Patricia trees (compressed tries).
- [ ] **Common examiner traps**: confusing tries with BSTs, forgetting terminal markers, writing wrong complexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Memory Aid for Exam Hall**
> 
> *"A keyword tree is a letter-by-letter filing cabinet for biological patterns — each branch is a letter, and walking down a branch is like tracing a motif in the genome."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suffix Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST743 - BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Suffix Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Suffix Trees — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Suffix Tree (Formal Definition):**
> A **Suffix Tree** $T$ for a non-empty string $S$ of length $\vert S \vert = n$ over a finite alphabet $\Sigma$ is a **rooted, directed tree** in which:
> 1. There are exactly $n$ **leaves**, numbered $1$ to $n$, one for each suffix $S[i..n]$, $1 \leq i \leq n$.
> 2. Every **internal node** (excluding the root) has at least **two children**.
> 3. Each **edge** is labeled with a non-empty substring of $S$.
> 4. The concatenation of edge labels along any path from the **root** to a leaf $i$ spells out the suffix $S[i..n]$.
> 5. **No two edges** out of the same node may begin with the same character (the **prefix-free / path-label-uniqueness property**).

A unique terminal character **\$** (which is lexicographically smaller than every character in $\Sigma$) is appended to $S$ so that no suffix is a prefix of another — this guarantees that every suffix terminates at a unique leaf.

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Metric |
|:------:|:--------|:----------------|
| $n$ | Length of the text $S$ | $\geq 1$ |
| $m$ | Length of the pattern $P$ | $1 \leq m \leq n$ |
| $\vert \Sigma \vert$ | Alphabet size (e.g., **4** for DNA, **20** for proteins) | DNA = **{A,C,G,T}** |
| $S[i..j]$ | Substring of $S$ from index $i$ to $j$ | $1$-indexed in KTU notation |
| $\$$ | Sentinel terminator (not in $\Sigma$) | Unique leaf marker |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "A Street Map of Every Possible Ending"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine you have a long novel. The novel is the string $S =$ `"BANANA"`. Now, you want a search index that can instantly tell you whether any query word — say `"ANA"` — appears in the book, and **how many times** and **at which positions**.
>
> A **Suffix Tree** is like building a **massive street directory for every possible ending** of the novel. The root is the *town centre*. Every distinct two-letter, three-letter, four-letter … final segment of the book gets its own road, and at the end of every road stands a tiny signpost showing the page number (the suffix index). If a query word exists in the book, the searcher simply walks down from the root following the letters of the query. If the walk succeeds, the leaves at the end of that path are the **answer**.
>
> In one word: a *Suffix Tree is a compressed trie of all suffixes of a text* — the ultimate suffix index.

### 1.3 Why Suffix Trees Matter in Bioinformatics

- **Human genome ≈ 3 billion bp**, but pattern queries (e.g., locate a gene marker, motif, or restriction site) must be answered in **sub-linear time**.
- Naïve string search takes $O(nm)$ — too slow for genomic workloads.
- A Suffix Tree answers substring queries in **$O(m)$** time *after* an $O(n)$ preprocessing step — a one-time investment that pays off for thousands of queries.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Viva:** "Suffix Tree = build once ($O(n)$), search many times ($O(m)$ each)."

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Components of a Suffix Tree

Let $S = x_1 x_2 \dots x_n \$$. The suffix tree $ST(S)$ has the following structural anatomy:

- **Root** — A virtual node with no incoming edge. It has at least two children (because of \$).
- **Branching (internal) nodes** — Vertices with $\geq 2$ children. Path-label is the string spelled from the root to that node.
- **Leaves** — Exactly $n$ leaves (one per suffix), each labeled with the suffix starting index.
- **Edge labels** — Non-empty substrings; stored compactly as **pointer pairs** $(i,j)$ referencing the original text (this is what makes the tree $O(n)$ space).

## 2.2 Core Properties (Board-Favourite Points)

| # | Property | Formal Statement | Engineering Implication |
|:-:|:---------|:-----------------|:------------------------|
| 1 | **Total nodes** | $\leq 2n$ | Linear space — feasible for genomes |
| 2 | **Total leaves** | Exactly $n$ | One leaf per suffix |
| 3 | **Height** | $\leq n$ | Worst case = all-same-character string |
| 4 | **Edge-label compression** | Each edge stored as $(start, end)$ pair | Constant space per edge |
| 5 | **Suffix-link** | Every internal node $v$ (except root) has a link $\mathrm{link}(v)$ pointing to the node representing the suffix of $v$'s path-label (with the first char removed) | Enables $O(n)$ construction |
| 6 | **Right-context (head/tail)** | During construction, only the right-end of edge labels changes | The key trick of Ukkonen's algorithm |

## 2.3 Construction Algorithms — From Naïve to Optimal

### 2.3.1 Naïve Algorithm — Build a Trie of All Suffixes
- Insert each of the $n$ suffixes into a trie one by one.
- Time = $O(n^2)$ in the worst case.

### 2.3.2 Weiner's Algorithm (1973) — First $O(n)$ Algorithm
- Builds the tree right-to-left.
- Complex to implement.

### 2.3.3 McCreight's Algorithm (1976) — Left-to-Right
- Uses **suffix links**.
- $O(n)$ time, $O(n)$ space.

### 2.3.4 Ukkonen's Algorithm (1995) — Online $O(n)$
- Constructs the tree **incrementally** by extending one character at a time.
- Introduces the **right-context (head/tail)** trick: store only the endpoint of edge labels during construction, then canonicalise at the end.
- Most commonly taught in KTU.

## 2.4 Pseudocode (Ukkonen's Algorithm — Online Construction)

```
ALGORITHM UkkonenBuild(S, n):
    Input : String S of length n, terminated with '$'
    Output: Suffix Tree of S

    1.  Create root node 'root'.
    2.  For i = 1 to n+1:                       // n+1 because of '$'
    3.      For j = 1 to i:
    4.          Apply Extension Rule 1, 2, or 3
    5.              Rule 1: leaf already exists  -> do nothing
    6.              Rule 2: split edge, add leaf
    7.              Rule 3: end reached, add new leaf
    8.  End For
    9.  End For
    10. Remove any '$' marker if not part of input
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Suffix Links (Active Pointers):**
> A suffix link from node $v$ (path-label $= x\alpha$) points to the node $w$ whose path-label is $\alpha$. These links make Ukkonen's algorithm $O(n)$ because each extension is amortised $O(1)$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Complexity Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Complexity | Standard Boundary |
|:--------|:---------------------|:------------------|
| Number of leaves | $n$ | $n \geq 1$ |
| Max number of nodes | $\leq 2n - 1$ | Tight bound |
| Space complexity | $O(n)$ | Assuming alphabet is constant |
| Construction (Naïve) | $O(n^2)$ | Worst case |
| Construction (Ukkonen / McCreight) | $O(n)$ | Worst case, $n$ = string length |
| Pattern search | $O(m)$ | After construction |
| Find all occurrences of $P$ | $O(m + z)$ | where $z$ = number of occurrences |
| Longest repeat | $O(n)$ | Tree traversal |
| Longest common substring of two strings | $O(n_1 + n_2)$ | Build GST of both |
| Shortest unique substring | $O(n)$ | DFS on tree |
| Total distinct substrings | $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | Counted by internal-node path labels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule of KTU Board Exams:**
> Always mention **time complexity**, **space complexity**, and **applicability to genomic data** when answering any algorithm question on suffix trees.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application Domain | Use of Suffix Trees |
|:-------------------|:--------------------|
| **Genomics** | Find motifs, SNPs, repeat elements |
| **Read Mapping (BWA-style)** | Indexing reference genome |
| **Plagiarism Detection** | Longest common substring across documents |
| **Data Compression (LZ77 / LZSS)** | Suffix trees are the theoretical engine |
| **Anti-Virus / Intrusion Detection** | Pattern matching against huge signature databases |
| **Bioinformatics Pipelines (BLAST alternatives)** | Faster exact-match prefiltering |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Build the Suffix Tree of $S =$ "BANANA\$"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Example for KTU 2024 Exams.** Memorise this step-by-step construction — at least one variant appears in every KTU ESE.

### 3.1.1 List All Suffixes

| Index $i$ | Suffix $S[i..n]$ |
|:---------:|:-----------------|
| 1 | `B A N A N A $` |
| 2 | `A N A N A $` |
| 3 | `N A N A $` |
| 4 | `A N A $` |
| 5 | `N A $` |
| 6 | `A $` |
| 7 | `$` |

Total = **7** suffixes ⇒ **7 leaves**.

### 3.1.2 Insertion Walk-through (Ukkonen-style)

**Step 1 — Insert suffix 1: `BANANA$`**

A single path from root spelling `BANANA$`, ending at leaf **1**.

**Step 2 — Insert suffix 2: `ANANA$`**

A new path from root spelling `ANANA$`, ending at leaf **2**. Root now has two children: edge labeled `B…` and edge labeled `A…`.

**Step 3 — Insert suffix 3: `NANA$`**

A new path from root spelling `NANA$`, ending at leaf **3**. Root has three children: `B…`, `A…`, `N…`.

**Step 4 — Insert suffix 4: `ANA$`**

Walk from root: `A` is already an edge, so go inside. Next character is `N`; create a new branch and leaf **4**.

**Step 5 — Insert suffix 5: `NA$`**

Walk from root: `N` is already an edge; the remaining `A$` requires a new branch, leaf **5**.

**Step 6 — Insert suffix 6: `A$`**

Walk from root: `A` exists. The string `A$` cannot continue — **split the edge** after `A`, add a new internal node, and leaf **6**.

**Step 7 — Insert suffix 7: `$`**

Add a new edge from root labeled `$` leading to leaf **7**.

### 3.1.3 Final Compressed Suffix Tree (after path compression)

The internal branching node `A` (under root) and the branching node `N` (after `A`) become shared sub-trees. The final tree is:

```
                        (root)
                       / | | \
                      B  A N  $
                     /  /| \
                    A  N $ A   (continues into shared subtree)
                   /
                  N
                 / \
                A   A
               /     \
              $       $   (leaves 2,4,6,7)
```

More precisely, the canonical edge-label representation:

| Edge (parent → child) | Edge Label | Leaves Reached |
|:----------------------|:-----------|:---------------|
| root → $v_B$ | `BA` | 1 |
| root → $v_{AN}$ | `A` | 2, 4, 6 |
| root → $v_N$ | `NA` | 3, 5 |
| $v_{AN}$ → leaf 2 | `NA$` | 2 |
| $v_{AN}$ → leaf 4 | `$` | 4 |
| $v_{AN}$ → leaf 6 | `$` | 6 |
| $v_N$ → leaf 3 | `NA$` | 3 |
| $v_N$ → leaf 5 | `$` | 5 |
| root → leaf 7 | `$` | 7 |

(Visual schematic is provided in Section 4.)

### 3.1.4 Verification of Properties

- Leaves = 7 = $n$ ✓
- Internal nodes ≤ 6 = $2n - 1$ ✓
- No two edges from same node share a first character ✓
- Every root-to-leaf path spells a suffix ✓

## 3.2 Analytical Search Procedure — Worked Walk-through

**Query:** Find all occurrences of pattern $P =$ `"ANA"` in $S =$ `"BANANA$"`.

### Step-by-step traversal

1. Start at **root**.
2. Follow edge labeled `A` (root → $v_{AN}$). 1 character matched, 2 to go.
3. From $v_{AN}$, no edge starts with `N` directly; descend into edge label `NA$` using **skip/count trick**, match 2 characters. 0 to go.
4. Match succeeded at node $v_{AN}$. Report all leaves in its sub-tree: **{2, 4, 6}**.
5. Conclusion: $P$ occurs at positions 2, 4, and 6 in $S$.

Total time = $O(m + z) = O(3 + 3) = O(6) = O(1)$ relative to $n$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Complexity Bound — Ukkonen's $O(n)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{cost of extension } i) \\
     &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} O(1) \quad \text{(amortised, by suffix-link argument)} \\
     &= O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic of amortisation:** Each extension performs at most one of the three rules, and suffix links guarantee that the "active point" advances monotonically. The active point is reset at most $n$ times. Hence total work is bounded by a constant multiple of $n$.

## 3.4 Derivation — Number of Distinct Substrings

Every distinct substring of $S$ corresponds to a unique path from the root in $ST(S)$. The number of paths equals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{substr}} &= \sum_{v \in \text{internal nodes}} \text{(sum of edge-label lengths from root to } v\text{)} \\
                  &= \frac{n(n+1)}{2} - \sum_{v \in \text{leaves}} \text{(path length from root to } v\text{)} + \text{(internal contribution)} \\
                  &\leq \frac{n(n+1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

In the KTU standard form, the answer accepted is:

$$
\boxed{\,N_{\text{distinct substrings}} = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\,}
$$

(For $S =$ "BANANA\$", $n=7$, hence at most 28 distinct substrings.)

## 3.5 Generalised Suffix Tree (GST) — for Multiple Strings

When comparing $k$ strings $S_1, S_2, \dots, S_k$ (e.g., two chromosomes, or a reference + a query read):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{GST}(S_1, S_2, \dots, S_k) &= \mathrm{ST}(S_1 \$_1 S_2 \$_2 \dots S_k \$_k) \\
\text{where } \$_i \notin \Sigma \text{ and } \$_i \neq \$_j
\end{aligned}
$$

> Each unique terminator makes the suffix of one string distinguishable from the suffixes of the others — a key trick for **multiple-sequence longest common substring** problems.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture Flow of Suffix Tree Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input String S of length n] --> B[Append Unique Sentinel Dollar]
    B --> C[Initialise Root Node]
    C --> D[For i = 1 to n plus 1]
    D --> E[For j = 1 to i]
    E --> F{Active Point Already in Tree?}
    F -- Yes Rule 1 --> G[Skip Extension Do Nothing]
    F -- No Splits Required Rule 2 --> H[Split Edge Add New Internal Node]
    F -- No Reaches End Rule 3 --> I[Extend Path Add New Leaf]
    G --> J[Update Suffix Link]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{All Extensions Done?}
    K -- No --> E
    K -- Yes --> L[Canonicalise Edge Labels]
    L --> M[Final Suffix Tree of S]
    M --> N[Ready for Pattern Queries]
    N --> O[Search Pattern P in O of m time]
    O --> P[Report Leaf Positions as Matches]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Suffix Tree Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Parent Node] --> B[Edge Label Tuple start end]
    B --> C[Current Node Internal]
    C --> D[Children Dictionary char to Child]
    C --> E[Suffix Link Pointer]
    C --> F[Path Label Spelled String]
    E --> G[Another Internal Node]
    C --> H[Leaves Index 1 to n]
    H --> I[Leaf 1]
    H --> J[Leaf 2]
    H --> K[Leaf k]
```

## 4.3 Concrete Suffix Tree for S equals BANANA Dollar

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root((Root)) --> B[B to A]
    Root --> AA[A]
    Root --> NN[N to A]
    Root --> Doll[Dollar]
    B --> L1[Leaf 1]
    AA --> AAin[A Internal]
    AAin --> L2[Leaf 2]
    AAin --> L4[Leaf 4]
    AAin --> L6[Leaf 6]
    NN --> L3[Leaf 3]
    NN --> L5[Leaf 5]
    Doll --> L7[Leaf 7]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The edge from root to node `A` is labeled with the single character `A` (used by suffixes starting at positions 2, 4, 6). The two-character edges `BA`, `NA` are also shown as compressed path-labels. Leaves carry the starting index of the corresponding suffix.

## 4.4 Decision Flow for a Pattern Search

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pattern P of length m] --> S[Start at Root of Suffix Tree]
    S --> Q{Does Edge from Current Node Start with Next Character of P?}
    Q -- No --> X[No Match Report Zero Occurrences]
    Q -- Yes --> T[Traverse Edge Matching Chars]
    T --> R{P Fully Matched?}
    R -- No --> Q
    R -- Yes --> L[Collect All Leaves in Subtree]
    L --> Z[Return Leaf Set as Match Positions]
```
